Top 3 Actions This Week
CRITICAL #1
Gen AI launch at delivery risk
LLM bus factor = 1. Sara Kumar is the sole L4 expert. Priya Shah and Harish Patel (C1 skill holders) are flight-risk. Approve 2 senior LLM hires this week or delay Q2 launch.
Owner: CEO · Due: this week
HIGH #2
Harish Patel retention exposed
Director of Engineering carries 3 critical skills at bus factor 1. Flight risk score 4.6/5. Previous retention action (equity refresh) moved risk only marginally (4.6→4.2). CEO-level intervention required.
Owner: CEO · Due: 14 days
HIGH #3
40% of workforce unassessed
18 employees and 22 roles still have no proficiency data. All headline RCGS/SCR metrics on this dashboard exclude them. Assign assessment owner this week — target 100% coverage by Q2 FY26.

Domain Averages
AI Synthesis — Executive Intelligence Layer
THE ONE-LINE READ
NovaTech is a financially elite, culturally strong organisation with a critically misaligned capability infrastructure — delivering top-quartile financial returns on a below-median L&D foundation that will break before the next growth phase completes.
Diagnostic — What All 44 Indices Say as a System
Across 10 domains, four structural patterns emerge. Pattern 1 — Financial outperformance built on a lean workforce: REI (96), NRI (95), RGI (91), and GMI (88) are all top-quartile — but every financial metric is a function of 50 people. The organisation has no financial buffer against a capability shock. Pattern 2 — Complete framework, incomplete execution: SCAI (100%) means the skills architecture is fully built. SCR (58%) means it is barely half-trained. ACI (40%) means only 40% of the workforce has been assessed. The org has invested in infrastructure without investment in evidence. Pattern 3 — Training volume without strategic alignment: LPI (92%) confirms 100% training completion. LAI (34%) confirms 66% of training hours are misaligned with critical skills. NovaTech is one of the best-trained organisations in the region at doing the wrong things. Pattern 4 — Client health masking CS capacity risk: CHI (88), CGI (84), CQI (82) are all green — but 72 clients supported by 12 CS staff is a ratio that will snap under the next wave of enterprise onboarding. The composite score of 74/100 places NovaTech in the Strong band — but the band conceals a 10-domain split: Financial/Client/Culture average 86/100 (top quartile); Skill/L&D/Assessment average 58/100 (below peer median). These are two organisations in one operating model, and the gap is growing.
90-Day Index-Driven Action Playbook
Week 1–2 · Immediate
LAI 34%→50% Reallocate 3 elective programs to LLM/Azure/Python — zero cost, +800 critical hours
SCR 58%→65% Launch LLM Bootcamp ($45K, 15 employees) — highest single-program impact
VCS 54%→62% Manager assessments for 4 single-source employees — lifts confidence immediately
ACI 40%→60% Self-assessment campaign for 10 unassessed employees
Month 1–3 · Priority
RCGS→+0.80 Dir. of Engineering succession plan + 6-month development track
CSI 82→87 Add 3 Cognitive competencies; one L4+ workshop with HRBP
DEI 71→76 Promote 2 senior women to Principal/Director in next cycle
OSHI 62→68 Consolidate 3 Prod Eng role titles; reduce from 35→32 distinct roles
CST 79→84 Hire 2 CS Associates → reduce accounts/CSM from 6.0 to 4.5
Quarter 2 · Protect & Scale
WSI 88% · WRI 83% Protect attrition floor — do not sacrifice stability for growth speed
Financial indices REI/NRI/GMI — maintain headcount discipline; no unplanned hires
DQI 100% Schedule Q2 QC cycle before 30 new assessment records ingested
BCI/DRI/KTI Run tabletop DRP exercise — lifts DRI from 32→58 with one session
Under Observation — Monitor Closely
—
Domain Intelligence · Analyst Layer
Domain 1 · Structural Health
Structural Health Indices
OSHI · RDI · LDI · SCI
AI Insight — Structural Health
OSHI at 62/100 signals moderate structural debt — the org is functional but accumulating fragility. RDI at 40/100 reflects role density at 0.70 (35 distinct titles for 50 employees), well above the healthy ceiling of 0.35. The dominant risk: as NovaTech scales from 50→100 employees, every new hire is added to a title structure that already has too many thin roles. SCI at 85 is the standout strength — management spans are healthy, preserving both communication speed and accountability clarity.
Domain 2 · Workforce & People
Workforce & People Indices
WSI · WRI · MHI · HAI · ATR · TEN
AI Insight — Workforce & People
Workforce indices are NovaTech's most consistently strong domain — all 6 above target. WSI (88%) and WRI (83%) confirm low attrition and resilient critical role coverage. HAI at 36% appears exceptional but requires a definitional note: NovaTech uses a broader HiPo definition than the industry standard 10–15%; normalised against a 25% ceiling it still scores green. The genuine concern sits in MHI (72/100) — the correlation between L2–L3 attrition at 14.2% and under-resourced management suggests junior engineers are leaving partly due to insufficient career support, not just market pressure.
Domain 3 · Skill Coverage
Skill Coverage Indices
SCAI · SCR · CCI · SDI · SVI
AI Insight — Skill Coverage
The 32-point gap between SCAI (100%) and SCR (58%) is the single most important number in this summary. It means NovaTech has a complete, well-designed skills framework — and then fails to train on 42% of the skills in it. SVI at 52/100 reveals the compounding problem: market demand for skills is accelerating faster than the org is adopting them. Every quarter this gap persists, the training deficit grows. CCI at 68% confirms the effect downstream: 32% of required proficiencies are not being met at role threshold, translating directly to delivery risk on technical engagements.
Domain 4 · Competency & Assessment
Competency & Assessment Indices
CSI · VCS · RREI · RCGS
AI Insight — Competency & Assessment
VCS at 54% is the most structurally concerning index in this domain. It reflects that the organisation's average validated capability — confirmed by at least 2 independent sources — sits below the peer median of 65%. CSI at 82 looks reasonable in isolation but is dragged below benchmark by weak Technical competency scores (0.70 vs 0.88 peer average). RREI shows moderate risk exposure — the Director of Engineering role carries a 4.8/5 RREI, meaning a single departure in that role creates a cascading delivery crisis. RCGS of +1.24 aggregates the skills gap across all 13 roles; the Director (+2.84) and VP Sales (+2.66) are the outliers pulling this number above target.
Domain 5 · L&D Effectiveness
L&D Effectiveness Indices
LAI · TDI · CRI · LPI · SKI
AI Insight — L&D Effectiveness
This is the lowest-scoring capability domain — and the one with the highest leverage. LAI at 34% means only 34 cents of every L&D dollar goes toward critical skills; 66 cents funds mandatory compliance and elective programs that don't move the capability dial. LPI at 92% (top quartile) confirms the training infrastructure is strong — completion rates, logistics, and delivery are excellent. The problem is not execution; it is strategic direction. CRI at 38% confirms the certification portfolio skews compliance-heavy. The fix is a portfolio rebalance, not new investment: redirecting 3 existing programs toward LLM, Azure, and Python costs zero additional budget and lifts LAI from 34% to an estimated 52% within one quarter.
Domain 6 · Financial Performance
Financial Performance Indices
RGI · GMI · OMI · NRI · REI
AI Insight — Financial Performance
Financial indices confirm NovaTech is operating at top-decile efficiency for a B2B SaaS company at this ARR stage. REI (Revenue Efficiency Index) at 96/100 reflects Revenue/Employee of $1.14M — more than double the peer median of $450K — enabled by the lean 50-person headcount model. NRI at 95/100 (NRR 118%) is the most strategically valuable metric: it means existing customers are spending 18% more year-on-year, creating a compounding growth engine that requires zero sales cost. The capability risk lands here too: REI at 96 is only sustainable if the same 50 people can continue delivering at this intensity.
Domain 7 · Client Health
Client Health Indices
CHI · CGI · CQI · CST
AI Insight — Client Health
All four client health indices are green — the strongest uniform-green domain alongside Financial. CHI at 88/100 reflects a rare combination where retention quality and growth are both accelerating. CST at 79/100 is the one index to watch — it is trending downward in H2 2024 as CS capacity stretches. 72 clients supported by 12 CS staff means 6 accounts per CSM, against the benchmark of 4–5 at this engagement complexity. CHI will follow CST downward within 2 quarters if CS headcount is not reinforced.
Domain 8 · ESG & Culture
ESG & Culture Indices
EEI · eNPS · DEI · ESI · PAI
AI Insight — ESG & Culture
ESG and Culture is the most improved domain over 5 years — eNPS tripled from 18 to 42, and EEI lifted from 3.4 to 4.1/5. The normalised eNPS Index at 84/100 places NovaTech in the top quartile globally. DEI at 71/100 is the amber flag: 50/50 gender parity at org level masks the 28% women-in-leadership figure — a 22pp drop-off that defines the current glass ceiling. ESI at 76 reflects genuine environmental progress — carbon down 19.7% while headcount grew 78%. PAI at 79 shows the 4.2% pay equity gap improving but still above Singapore's 2% statutory target.
Domain 9 · Data & Governance
Data & Governance Indices
DQI · ACI · TAI
AI Insight — Data & Governance
DQI at 100% — all 60 QC checks passing — is the foundation on which all other L1 indices rest. Without data quality, every other number in this summary is suspect. ACI at 40% is the most critical governance risk: only 20 of 50 employees have proficiency assessments, meaning 60% of the workforce is capability-unknown. Every index in Domain 3, 4, and 5 is computed on a sample of 40% of the workforce — a significant confidence limitation. Closing ACI to 80%+ would increase confidence in all other indices by approximately 28%.
Domain 10 · Org Resilience & Continuity BCP · DRP
Resilience & Continuity Indices
BCI · DRI · KTI — all three critical
Single Points of Failure & Succession Depth
Critical Role Continuity Register
8 SPOFs · succession depth · RTO · knowledge status · DRP status
Critical RoleSPOF?Current HolderSuccessor 1Successor 2RTO (weeks)Knowledge DocumentedDRP TestedRisk
Geographic Capability Concentration Risk
Critical skills by location
Project–Skill Dependency & SPOF Exposure
Which projects depend on SPOF-held skills
Skill Scarcity & External Replaceability
DRP: how quickly can each critical skill be sourced externally?
Attrition Risk by Business Unit
DRP trigger probability
BCP & DRP Action Plan
Business Continuity Plan — Priorities
Prevention
Disaster Recovery Plan — Response Protocols
Response
AI Insight — Org Resilience & Continuity
All three resilience indices are red — BCI (48/100), DRI (32/100), and KTI (38/100) — making Domain 10 the lowest-scoring domain in the entire L1 summary. The revenue machine is running, but it has no tested recovery plan if a critical component fails. BCI at 48 means NovaTech is approximately 6–8 weeks from a delivery crisis if any of its 8 SPOFs departs without notice. DRI at 32 is the most alarming: zero of the 13 critical roles have a tested disaster recovery protocol. KTI at 38 confirms 11 of 18 C1+C2 skills are locked in single individuals with no documented transfer artefacts. Three-action minimum before further scaling: (1) Document top 5 SPOF roles within 60 days; (2) Name Primary and Backup successors for all 13 critical roles within 90 days; (3) Run one tabletop DRP exercise before Q2 2025 — this alone lifts DRI from 32 to an estimated 58.

AI Insight — Org Overview · SIP Architect
Analysis based on Organisation Setup Blocks A, B, D, H, I, J, K, L, M
What's Strong
Revenue CAGR 32.7% (2020–2024)
Well above the ~20% SaaS industry median. Revenue per employee at $1.14M places NovaTech in the top quartile globally.
🏢 Block A · Org Identity
Workforce Stability — WSI 88%, Attrition 8%
5-year low attrition rate. eNPS at 42 (industry avg 28) signals a culture-driven, stable workforce.
🏢 Block A · Performance Data 🏗️ Block B · Hierarchy
Training Volume — 131.4h/employee
3.3× above industry minimum. 821 training records with 100% completion rate. Infrastructure is strong.
📦 Block D · Services & Training
Level Pyramid — Execution-Heavy (L3 at 40%)
Healthy shape for a growth-stage firm. SCI at 85/100 — avg 6.25 reports/manager is within optimal range.
🏗️ Block B · Hierarchy Architecture 📋 Block L · Role Definitions
What's At Risk — and How AI Detected It
1. SCR 58% — 28 Skills Have Zero Training Coverage
CRITICAL
Detection: AI cross-referenced Block J (60 skills in the Skills Dictionary) against Block D (training programs linked to services) and Block H (C1–C4 criticality scale). Result: 28 skills — including Python, Azure, LLM — are defined as required but have zero training program mapped. 8 of these are rated C1 (Mission Critical).
📖 Block J · Skills Dictionary 📦 Block D · Services ⚖️ Block H · Criticality
2. CSI 0.82 — Competency Gap Widening vs Industry
HIGH
Detection: AI evaluated Block K (14 competencies across Core, Behavioural, Leadership, Technical) against the proficiency thresholds in Block I, then benchmarked against Block A industry context (IT & Software). Technical competency scores 0.70 vs 0.88 peer average. Cognitive competencies also below median.
🧭 Block K · Competencies 📏 Block I · Proficiency Levels 🏢 Block A · Industry Context
3. RDI 0.70 — Role Title Fragmentation
HIGH
Detection: AI computed Role Density = Distinct Roles (Block L: 35 role profiles) ÷ Headcount (Block A: 50 employees) = 0.70. Healthy ceiling is 0.35. This means nearly every employee has a unique title, creating accountability fragmentation, slowing role→skill mapping, and increasing governance overhead as the org scales.
📋 Block L · Role Definitions 🏗️ Block B · Hierarchy 🏢 Block A · Headcount
Recommended Immediate Actions
The workforce is stable (WSI 88%, attrition 8%) and culture is strong (eNPS 42). This creates a rare execution window — act now before the pre-IPO scaling phase compresses available time.
Week 1–2 · Immediate
Close Critical Skill Gaps
Reallocate 3 elective training programs to LLM, Python, Azure. Zero additional budget — redirects 800+ hours to C1-critical skills. Lifts SCR from 58% → ~68%.
Create Skill Gap Project
Month 1 · Priority
Assess the Unknown 60%
Launch self-assessment campaign for 30 unassessed employees. Lifts Breadth Score confidence from 40% → 80% and sharpens all downstream indices.
Launch Assessment Cycle
Month 1–3 · Structural
Consolidate Role Titles
Merge 35 distinct roles → ~25 by combining similar titles in Prod Engineering. Drops RDI from 0.70 → 0.50. Simplifies every downstream skill mapping.
Review Scope Management

Bus Factor — Single Points of Failure
Bus Factor Distribution
Employees at L3+ per critical skill
Bus Factor = number of employees at Advanced (L3) or Expert (L4) proficiency. A value of 1 means if that person leaves, the skill is effectively lost. Target: ≥3 for all C1 critical skills.
Critical Bus Factor Risks
C1/C2 skills · Bus factor ≤ 2
SkillCritL4 ExpertsL3+ TotalBus FactorRisk
Forecasted Skill Demand — Tied to Product Roadmap
Current vs 12M vs 24M Demand
Top 10 critical skills · role slots needed
Demand Drivers
Product roadmap commitments
Gen AI Module Launch · Q2 FY26
Requires +8 LLM, +6 Prompt Eng, +5 MLOps engineers. Gap: 13 roles unfilled.
Analytics v2 Platform · Q3 FY26
Requires +4 Data Eng, +3 Python, +2 Azure. Gap: 6 roles unfilled.
Mobile App Relaunch · Q4 FY26
Requires +3 React Native, +2 Node.js. Gap: 4 roles unfilled.
Pre-IPO Compliance · FY27
Requires +2 SOX, +1 InfoSec Audit, +1 Data Privacy. Gap: 4 roles unfilled.

AI Insight — Skill Inventory · SIP Architect
Analysis based on Organisation Setup Blocks J, H, L, D, E, G
What's Strong
SCAI 100% — Complete Skill-to-Role Mapping
All 60 skills are mapped to at least 1 role via 189 Role-Skill Requirements. The taxonomy architecture is fully built — a rare achievement at this org size.
📖 Block J · Skills Dictionary 📋 Block L · Role Definitions
Taxonomy Growth — 31 → 60 Skills (94% in 5 years)
Skill catalogue nearly doubled since 2020, covering 11 domains. Emerging skills (LLM, GenAI, MLOps) proactively flagged — the org is forward-looking.
📖 Block J · Taxonomy 🌍 Block E · Market Demand
Criticality Framework Fully Defined (C1–C4)
All 60 skills have criticality ratings assigned. 8 C1 (Mission Critical) + 10 C2 (High Impact) clearly identified — enables prioritisation of every L&D investment.
⚖️ Block H · Criticality Scale
Regulatory Skills Covered — ISO, GDPR, PDPA, POSH
4 regulatory skills catalogued with compliance-linked training. 100% completion rate on mandatory compliance programs across all 50 employees.
📖 Block J · Skills Dictionary 📦 Block D · Services
What's At Risk — and How AI Detected It
1. SCR 58% — Training Coverage Has Not Kept Pace With Taxonomy Growth
CRITICAL
Detection: AI matched Block J (60 skills) against Block D (training programs in services). Result: only 32 of 60 skills have an active training program. The 28 uncovered skills include LLM (scarcity 0.97), Python (demand 16 roles), Azure Cloud (demand 15 roles) — all rated C1/C2 in Block H. The taxonomy doubled but training investment stayed flat.
📖 Block J · Skills Dictionary 📦 Block D · Services ⚖️ Block H · Criticality
2. Emerging Skill Scarcity — LLM at 0.97, ML at 0.88
CRITICAL
Detection: AI computed scarcity = 1 – (employees with skill ÷ roles requiring skill) from Block J proficiency data × Block L role requirements. LLM: 4 employees vs 18 roles needing it = 0.97 scarcity. ML: 6 vs 14 = 0.88. Azure: 9 vs 15 = 0.60. These three skills account for 72% of the Gen AI product's delivery risk per Block D project data.
📖 Block J · Proficiency Data 📋 Block L · Role Requirements 📦 Block D · Projects
3. Technical Skills Under-Represented — 36.7% vs 42–48% Industry Norm
HIGH
Detection: AI categorised all 60 skills from Block J by type (Technical 22, Functional 23, Behavioural 11, Regulatory 4), then benchmarked against Block A industry (IT & Software). Technical at 36.7% is 5–11pp below the SaaS peer median of 42–48%. Behavioural skills are over-represented at 18.3% for a product company — indicating the taxonomy was built around existing strengths rather than strategic needs.
📖 Block J · Skill Types 🏢 Block A · Industry Context
4. Tool Migration Risk — AutoCAD Sunsetting, Kubernetes Not Adopted
MEDIUM
Detection: AI cross-referenced Block G (tool lifecycle flags) with Block J (skills linked to tools). AutoCAD is marked "Sunsetting within 12m" but 12 employees still list it as a primary skill. Kubernetes is "Planned Adoption" but has zero proficiency records. Skills linked to migrating tools need retraining pathways.
🔧 Block G · Tools & Tech 📖 Block J · Tool-Linked Skills
Recommended Immediate Actions
The skill taxonomy is architecturally complete (SCAI 100%) — the foundation is solid. The gap is in training activation and proficiency verification. Every action below leverages existing infrastructure, not new investment.
Week 1–2 · Immediate
Commission LLM + Python + Azure Training
Create training programs for top 3 scarcity skills. Redirect budget from 3 low-impact elective programs. Lifts SCR from 58% → 63% and directly de-risks Gen AI launch.
Create Training Project
Month 1 · Priority
Rebalance Taxonomy — Add 6 Technical Skills
Add Kubernetes, Terraform, CI/CD, API Design, MLOps, Data Engineering to Block J. Moves Technical from 36.7% → 42%, aligning with SaaS industry median.
Edit Skills Dictionary
Month 1–2 · Verification

Leadership Competency Depth — Single Points of Failure
Competency Bus Factor
Employees at Expert (L4+) per critical competency
Bus Factor = number of employees demonstrating the competency at Advanced/Expert level. A value of 1 means loss of that person collapses the capability org-wide.

AI Insight — Competency Inventory
CSI of 0.82 indicates NovaTech scores 18% below industry benchmark overall. Cognitive and Technical competencies (2 each, 8% each) are severely under-represented for a product engineering company — peer SaaS firms average 20%+ coverage per category. Recommended: Add 3 Cognitive competencies (Systems Thinking, Data-Driven Judgement, Hypothesis Testing) and elevate Technical competency proficiency targets at L4–L5.

AI Insight — Employee Skill Profiles
Only 20 of 50 employees (40%) have proficiency data. Avg breadth is 0.54 — employees cover barely half their required skills. Sara Kumar and Uma Malik are at full breadth (1.0) with depth 3.8–3.9 — the org's strongest capability anchors. Pooja Verma (0.29), Swathi Brown (0.33), Meera Singh (0.38) are most at-risk. Actions: (1) Complete assessments for 30 remaining employees; (2) Prioritise L&D for 8 employees with breadth <0.50; (3) Deploy Uma Malik and Sara Kumar as LLM/ML peer mentors.

AI Insight — Training Program Mapping
SCR of 58% means 28 skills — including Python, Azure, React, Node.js, ETL, LLM — have zero training coverage. These are NovaTech's highest-demand technical skills. Mandatory compliance training covers only 3 skills but generates 35% of all records. Recommend: Commission 5 new technical programs targeting Python, Azure, LLM, React/Node.js, and BI Tools to lift SCR to 72%.

12 programs have no pre/post measurement
67% of the $180K training budget ($120K) is spent on programs with no effectiveness data. Completion ≠ learning. Recommendation: instrument all C1/C2 critical-skill programs with pre-assessment, 30-day post-assessment, and 90-day on-the-job application check.

AI Insight — Training Records
100% completion across all 821 records is exceptional but warrants scrutiny — bulk completion entry is likely rather than tracked individual completion. Product Engineering (316 records, 38.5%) dominates participation. GDPR, DEI, and POSH each have exactly 50 completions — one per employee — confirming mandatory all-hands programs. Recommend: Introduce post-training assessments to validate actual skill uplift vs recorded completion.

Hidden RCGS Impact
Vacant roles are excluded from the RCGS average shown at the top of this page. If included (treating current proficiency as 0), org-wide RCGS would be +2.18 instead of +1.24. The 3 critical vacancies alone account for +0.94 of hidden gap.

AI Insight — Role–Skill Matrix
The Role–Skill Matrix reveals a structural capability inversion: the most senior and highest-impact roles carry the largest gaps, while junior and specialist roles meet or exceed their requirements. Director of Engineering (+2.84) and VP Sales (+2.66) — the two roles with greatest revenue delivery accountability — are simultaneously the two roles furthest from required proficiency. This is not a coincidence; it reflects a hiring strategy that brought talent in at the right experience level but did not invest in sustained skills development at the leadership layer. The role family picture is equally stark: Engineering averages RCGS +1.42, Sales +1.83 — both above the critical threshold — while Customer Success (+0.62) and Corporate Functions (+0.28) are well-managed. The level progression pattern shows gap increasing monotonically from L2 (+0.6) to L6 (+2.75) — meaning NovaTech is promoting people into senior roles faster than it is building the skills those roles demand. This compounds annually as each promoted employee carries forward the same skill gaps, now in a higher-stakes context. The three highest-ROI interventions: (1) Director of Engineering — immediate 90-day targeted coaching on Python, AWS, DevOps, ML (4 specific skills, all L4 required); (2) Engineering Manager — leadership skills investment at L3→L4 proficiency (the highest RREI combined with a vacancy risk); (3) ML Engineer roles — LLM and MLOps coverage which is currently absent from the team's assessed skills despite the Gen AI module being live in production.

Top 7 Critical Skill Gaps — What Leadership Must Know
The 7 skills blocking FY25 delivery
#SkillGapHCBlocked InitiativeClosure PathAnnual Cost
1LLM / Gen AI3.208Gen AI Module GABUY$920k
2MLOps2.808Gen AI scalingBUY$640k
3Kubernetes / K8s2.4012Cloud Migration P2BORROW$480k
4Enterprise Negotiation2.309Enterprise pipelineBUILD$420k
5AWS Architecture2.1013Cloud MigrationBUILD$320k

AI Insight — Executive Summary
NovaTech's skill position can be stated in one sentence: the organization is operating at 64% of the role-required skill proficiency it defined for itself (2.24 achieved / 3.48 required). Seven skills are currently blocking FY25 roadmap commitments, costing the business $3.27M annually through revenue delay, contractor premiums, and delivery rework. The top 2 items (LLM and MLOps) cannot be closed through training because no internal expert exists to teach from — these require hiring. The remaining 5 can be closed through a mix of training and contracting at $400k total. The full skill closure plan costs $760k against $3.27M of avoided cost, producing a 4.3x ROI. See Competency Gap Analysis for the parallel competency dimension, which adds $1.51M of leadership and culture-related cost and brings the combined picture to $4.8M.
2 · Business Context — Why Skill Gaps Matter Right Now
Strategic Initiatives Driving Skill Requirements
7 FY25 initiatives · skills they depend on · current coverage
InitiativeTimelineSkills RequiredCoverageGap Severity
Gen AI Module GAQ2 FY25LLM, RAG, Prompt Eng, Vector DB28%Severe
Cloud Migration Phase 2Q3 FY25Terraform Adv, K8s, Service Mesh40%Severe
Enterprise GTMQ1 FY25Enterprise Negotiation, Strategic Accounts35%Severe
Global Expansion EMEAQ4 FY25Localization, Cross-Cultural Selling15%Severe
Platform Re-ArchitectureQ3 FY25System Design, Event Sourcing55%High
SOC2 / ISOQ2 FY25Security, Compliance, IAM50%High
IPO ReadinessQ4 FY25IR Comms, Financial Modeling25%Severe
Why now: 4 of 7 FY25 initiatives have skill coverage below 40%. At current closure velocity, 0 of 7 critical skill gaps will close before their business-required date. This analysis was triggered by the realization that the FY25 roadmap is not capability-feasible at current skill levels.
AI Insight — Business Context
The business context converts this from a skills report into a roadmap-feasibility report. Every critical skill gap on this page maps directly to a named FY25 initiative with a board-committed delivery date. LLM and MLOps map to the Gen AI GA. Kubernetes and AWS map to Cloud Migration Phase 2. Enterprise Negotiation maps to the ARR target. These are not abstract "nice-to-have" skill investments — they are prerequisites for commitments the company has already made. The analysis exists because the gap between what was committed and what can be delivered is now visible and quantifiable.
3 · Scope of Analysis
Coverage
10 departments · 13 roles · 50 employees · 5 offices (SF, Bangalore, Austin, NYC, London) · FY2024
Skill Inventory
58 skills across 11 domains: Backend, Frontend, Infrastructure, Cloud, ML, Data, Sales, Product, Marketing, Cross-functional, Security
Assessment Methods
Self-assessment · Manager evaluation · Peer 360° · Work-sample tests · Outcome-based validation (deal rates, code review, SLOs) · VCS: 54%
4 · Skills Framework — Measurement System
Proficiency Scale & RCGS Formula
How skill gaps are measured
5-Level Proficiency Scale:
L1Beginner — awareness only, cannot perform independently L2Foundational — can perform with guidance L3Proficient — independent performance, standard quality L4Advanced — teaches others, handles edge cases L5Expert — defines standards, innovates, recognized authority
RCGS Formula:
RCGS = Avg(Required Proficiency - Current Proficiency) per role

Positive = Gap (skills below requirement)
Negative = Exceeds (skills above requirement)
Target: RCGS ≤ +0.50
Current: RCGS +1.24 (148% above target)
Weighted Gap = Gap × Headcount affected. This converts a per-person gap into an org-level investment signal.
58 Skills by Domain
11 domains · count per domain
Skills by Criticality Tag
Critical · High · Medium · Low
AI Insight — Skills Framework
The framework carries an important structural observation: 18 of 58 skills are tagged business-critical, but only 7 of those 18 are fully covered at the required proficiency level. This means 61% of the skills the organization has designated as critical to its operations are currently below the proficiency bar the organization itself set. The framework is well-designed — 11 domains, 5 proficiency levels, clear gap formula — but the coverage against it is where the deficit lives. The measurement system is honest; the capability position it reveals is not comfortable.

AI Insight — Current Skills (AS-IS)
The 58-skill table reveals a pattern that the RCGS average conceals: the organization is not uniformly weak — it has pockets of genuine strength surrounded by significant deficits. Docker (3.20), React (3.10), Agile/Scrum (3.10) all exceed their targets, indicating the org invested effectively in foundational dev practices. But the skills the FY25 roadmap depends on — LLM (0.60), MLOps (1.00), Kubernetes (1.40), Enterprise Negotiation (1.50) — sit at the bottom of the proficiency distribution. The second concerning signal is assessment confidence: 14 of 58 skills are assessed at below 50% confidence, meaning they rely on single-source self-report. LLM is at 0% confidence — the reported gap of 3.20 is inferred from absence of measurement, not from actual assessment. The AS-IS picture is honest but approximately 30% of it sits on directional rather than validated evidence.

AI Insight — Required Skills (TO-BE)
The TO-BE picture reveals that the required proficiency bar rises steeply with seniority: L2 requires 2.80, L6 requires 4.50. This 1.70-point spread across 4 levels means each promotion adds roughly 0.43 points of new skill expectations, but the AS-IS data from Section 5 shows that actual proficiency decreases at higher levels (L6 actual is 1.75 vs L2 actual 2.20). The gap between TO-BE and AS-IS widens at every level, which is the structural finding from Section 8. The 6 emerging skills (RAG, Vector DBs, Prompt Engineering, Agent Orchestration, Cultural Fluency EMEA, IR Communication) are particularly concerning because they are 100% gap — no employee has been assessed on any of them, and no training path, certification, or hiring req currently references them.
7 · Skill Gap Identification — The Core Analysis

Pareto Concentration — 80% of Gap in 14 Skills
Cumulative weighted gap
AI Insight — Skill Gaps (7A)
The master gap table produces the single most actionable finding in this section: 80% of the org's total weighted skill gap is concentrated in just 14 of 58 skills. A focused 14-skill training and hiring catalog closes four-fifths of the deficit without touching the remaining 13 below-target skills. The top 4 (K8s, AWS, LLM, MLOps) are all infrastructure and ML skills tied to the Gen AI and Cloud Migration initiatives — they account for 27% of the total gap alone. Enterprise Negotiation at position 5 is the highest-weighted non-technical skill and carries more weighted gap than Python or DevOps because it sits in a role family with higher per-incumbent business impact.
7B · Skill Gap by Department
Department Skill RCGS Ranking
10 departments · ranked by skill gap

Where skill deficits concentrate by function
Department Skill Scorecard

DepartmentHCSkill RCGSWeighted GapTop Skill GapCritical SkillsStatus

Data / ML3+1.604.8LLM, MLOps2 (LLM, MLOps)Critical
Engineering18+1.4225.6K8s, AWS3 (K8s, AWS, Sys Design)Critical
Product5+1.105.5Product Analytics0At Risk
Marketing4+0.883.5SEO Analytics0At Risk
Customer Success6+0.623.7Technical Troubleshoot0At Risk
Operations1+0.400.4Process Automation0Healthy
Finance2+0.280.6FP&A Modeling0Healthy
HR / People2-0.10-0.2none0Exceeds
Legal0n/an/an/an/aOutsourced
AI Insight — Department Gap (7B)
Three departments carry 7 of 7 critical skill gaps: Engineering (3 critical: K8s, AWS, System Design), Sales (2 critical: Enterprise Negotiation, Strategic Accounts), and Data/ML (2 critical: LLM, MLOps). Together these three departments account for 77% of the org's total weighted skill gap (46.9 of 60.6 total gap-points). The remaining 7 departments combined carry 23% of gap and zero critical items. This is not a distributed problem — it is a three-department problem, and the training and hiring budget should concentrate accordingly rather than spreading across all 10 departments. Engineering carries the highest absolute weighted gap (25.6) because of its headcount (18), while Sales carries the highest per-capita RCGS (+1.83) because every sales role is below target on the enterprise motion.

AI Insight — Role Gap (7C)
The role-level view reveals a capability inversion: the two roles with the largest skill gaps (Director of Engineering +2.84, VP Sales +2.66) are also the two roles with the highest business impact. In a healthy organization, leadership roles show smaller gaps because incumbents have had more development time. NovaTech's inversion reflects a hiring strategy that brought talent at the right experience level but did not invest in the specific technical and domain skills those roles now require. Six of 13 roles carry critical skill gaps, concentrating in the L4-L6 band. Three roles exceed their targets (DevOps, Data Engineering, Talent Acquisition) and should be studied as reference models for what healthy role coverage looks like.
7D · Skill Gap by Level — The Promotion Debt
Required vs Current vs Gap by Level
L2 through L6 · the widening deficit
Monotonic Skill Gap Growth L2 → L6
+0.43 per level · the promotion debt curve
Level Skill Detail
5 levels
LevelHCRequiredCurrentSkill GapYoY Trend
L6 · Director34.501.75+2.75Worsening
L5 · Principal / Mgr54.202.10+2.10Worsening
L4 · Lead / Senior153.802.30+1.50Stable
L3 · Senior Associate203.302.40+0.90Stable
L2 · Associate72.802.20+0.60Improving
AI Insight — Level Gap (7D)
This is the most structurally important finding in the entire skill analysis. Skill gap grows monotonically from L2 (+0.60) to L6 (+2.75), adding +0.43 points at every promotion level. This means NovaTech promotes people faster than it develops their skills. Each promotion carries forward the unmet skill expectations of the previous level and adds new ones on top, compounding the debt. At L6, the Director of Engineering operates at 39% of the skill proficiency their own role definition requires (1.75 / 4.50). This is not an individual performance problem — it is a systemic promotion mechanic that does not gate on demonstrated skill closure. The fix is structural (a skill-gated promotion policy at L4→L5 where the curve steepens), not training-based.

Employee Gap Score Distribution

AI Insight — Individual Employee Gaps (7E)
The individual view converts the aggregate numbers into specific people who need specific interventions. The top 2 individuals (E001 Director of Engineering at +2.84, E007 VP Sales at +2.66) carry personal skill gaps larger than the org average, which means these two executives need personalized coaching plans, not group programs. The employee distribution shows a long tail: 38 of 50 employees have some skill gap, but only 13 have gaps above +1.0 (the threshold where individual intervention is more effective than catalog enrollment). The remaining 25 sit between 0 and +1.0 where standard LMS pathways and stretch assignments are sufficient. The tenure pattern at the individual level confirms the structural finding: 5 of the top 8 individuals have 3+ years of tenure, meaning their gaps accumulated over time through the promotion mechanic rather than arriving with them at hire. Employees E005 and E006 (Staff Engineers, 5yr and 6yr tenure) are the clearest examples — they were likely at-target when hired and have drifted as their role definition expanded underneath them.
7F · Skill Gap by Location
Skill RCGS by Office
5 offices · ranked by gap severity
Office × Critical Skill Heatmap
Where each critical skill physically sits
Office Skill Scorecard — All 5 Locations
HC · RCGS · top gap · strength · strategic role
OfficeHCSkill RCGSTop Skill GapStrengthStrategic RoleStatus
London3+2.20Enterprise Neg, Cross-Cultural SellingNone yetEMEA expansion (Q4)Critical · understaffed
SF HQ22+1.48Strategic skills, System DesignLeadership depth, ProductHQ · exec + productCritical · volume
Austin6+1.35Python Adv, AWSMid-level eng executionBackend + data engAt Risk
Bangalore14+1.12LLM, MLOpsK8s, Terraform, infra depthInfrastructure + ML scalingAt Risk · fragile
NYC5+0.92Enterprise NegotiationSales pipeline, CSEnterprise revenueAt Risk
Geographic fragility flag: the Bangalore office holds 3 critical infrastructure skills (K8s, Terraform, Service Mesh) through a single Senior DevOps Lead. A single departure from that office would collapse 3 concurrent FY25 initiatives. Geographic concentration is operational risk, not just HR risk.
AI Insight — Skill Gap by Location (7F)
The location view surfaces two patterns that department-level and role-level cuts cannot. First, London has the worst skill RCGS (+2.20) but carries the most strategically important FY25 mandate (EMEA expansion Q4). A 3-person office is expected to open a new region, and that office is the least-capable location in the entire company. This is a hiring problem, not a training problem — the location needs 3-5 additional senior hires before Q4, and the current London hiring pipeline is 8 weeks behind schedule. Second, Bangalore's capability profile is dangerously concentrated: the office holds 3 critical infrastructure skills (K8s, Terraform, Service Mesh) through a single Senior DevOps Lead. The office-level RCGS of +1.12 looks moderate, but the bus-factor-adjusted risk is severe because a single departure collapses three infrastructure capabilities simultaneously. The remaining locations (SF HQ, Austin, NYC) are within normal distribution for their sizes. Recommended geographic action: formalize London hiring acceleration (Move 6 in the FY25 plan), start the Bangalore K8s knowledge-transfer program in Week 1 of FY25 Q1 to de-risk the bus-factor 1 situation, and consider a secondary hub in EMEA to reduce London single-city dependency.
7G · Skill Gap by Tenure Band
Skill Gap by Tenure Band
4 bands · the U-shape pattern
Time-to-Proficiency Curve — New Hire → Full Contribution
NovaTech vs industry benchmark
Tenure Band Skill Scorecard
All 4 bands · diagnosis · action lever
Tenure BandHC% of OrgSkill GapDiagnosisAction Lever
0-1 year (new hires)1428%+1.82Onboarding debt — curriculum 22 months stale, new hires training against FY23 role definitionsP09 Onboarding Curriculum Refresh
1-3 years (growth cohort)1938%+1.25Healthiest cohort — development infrastructure worked for this group · most flight-proneRetention + stretch programs (T4 HRBP)
3-5 years (established)1224%+0.88Normal distribution · skills adequate for current rolePromotion-path development
5+ years (veterans)510%+1.55Role drift — role definition evolved, expectations never re-contracted with veteransT7 Veteran re-contracting conversation + targeted upskilling
AI Insight — Skill Gap by Tenure (7G)
The tenure view reveals a U-shaped curve that is structurally different from the promotion-level curve. The 0-1 year cohort (14 people, highest gap at +1.82) is underwater because the onboarding curriculum is 22 months stale — they are being trained on the FY23 role, not the FY25 role. The 5+ year cohort (5 people, +1.55 gap) is underwater for the opposite reason: they are trained on what the role was when they joined, and nobody has re-contracted with them as the role evolved. Between these two edges, the 1-3 year cohort (19 people, 38% of the org) is the healthiest cohort — which is also why it is the most flight-prone. This cohort represents the org's current development success and also its largest retention risk. The operational implication is that tenure is an actionable lens for intervention targeting: P09 Onboarding Refresh addresses the 0-1 year band, HRBP conversation T4 addresses the 1-3 year retention, HRBP conversation T7 addresses the 5+ year veteran drift. Each intervention targets a different tenure band with a different mechanism. Treating the org as one homogeneous population misses the fact that tenure is the variable that most explains the gap variance.
7H · Critical Skill Gaps — Cross-Dimensional View (Dept · Role · Level · Location)

Critical Skills by Department
Count of critical gaps per dept
Critical Skills by Role
Count per role
Critical Skills by Level
Count per level
AI Insight — Critical Gaps Cross-Dimensional (7F)
The cross-dimensional view shows that critical skill gaps cluster in predictable ways: Engineering carries 5 of 7 critical skills, L4-L5 carries all 7, and the ML Engineer and Senior Engineer roles are the two most over-represented roles across the critical list. This is operationally useful because it means the critical gap closure program has a clear population target: 12-15 people in Engineering and Sales at L4-L5 are the individuals who need to be in programs P01, P02, P03, and P06. The bus factor column adds urgency: LLM and MLOps are at bus factor 0 (nobody in the organization holds these skills at the required level), K8s and Enterprise Negotiation are at bus factor 1 (one departure collapses the capability), and AWS and System Design are at bus factor 2 (fragile but not single-point).
8 · Skill Gap Insights — Patterns, Root Causes, Risks
PATTERN 1 · Concentration
77% of weighted skill gap lives in 3 departments (Eng + Sales + Data/ML). 14 skills carry 80% of total gap. 6 of 13 roles carry critical gaps. The skill problem is concentrated, not distributed — and the investment should follow the same pattern.
Root cause: Revenue functions were staffed for growth potential; skill requirements grew faster than hiring criteria.
PATTERN 2 · Promotion Debt
Skill gap grows +0.43 per promotion level from L2 (+0.60) to L6 (+2.75). Promotions outrun development. Each level carries forward unclosed expectations and adds new ones. The debt compounds.
Root cause: Promotion gated on delivery performance, not demonstrated skill closure. No skill-proficiency requirement in the promotion criteria.
PATTERN 3 · Fragility
2 critical skills at bus factor 0 (LLM, MLOps — nobody holds them). 2 at bus factor 1 (K8s held by one Sr DevOps Lead in Bangalore, Ent Negotiation by VP Sales who is also in the gap). Single departure in K8s collapses infra for 3 initiatives simultaneously.
Root cause: Key-person risk accepted as normal operating condition. No knowledge-transfer policy.
PATTERN 4 · Measurement Blind Spots
14 of 58 skills assessed at below 50% confidence. LLM and Cross-Cultural Selling at 0% (never assessed). 6 emerging skills not in the inventory at all. The skill picture is incomplete — roughly 30% of it is directional rather than validated.
Root cause: Assessment infrastructure focuses on established skills. New skills enter the roadmap before entering the assessment system.
AI Insight — Skill Gap Insights
The four patterns together tell a single coherent story: NovaTech's skill gaps are concentrated in the roles and departments that carry the most business impact, compounded by a promotion system that does not gate on skill readiness, made fragile by single-person dependencies, and partially invisible to the current measurement system. The insight that ties them together is that these are not four independent problems — they are four symptoms of one organizational pattern: the company grew the business faster than it grew the people, and the systems that should have caught the gap (promotion criteria, knowledge transfer policies, assessment coverage) were not designed for the growth rate the company achieved. Training addresses the symptoms. Structural intervention addresses the cause. Both must run simultaneously for the $760k skill closure investment to produce outcomes that persist past 6 months.
9 · Business Impact — What Skill Gaps Cost in Revenue and Risk
Revenue Delay
$1.85M
Gen AI slip + enterprise deals
Contractor Premium
$920k
K8s, AWS, SRE sourced externally
Delivery Rework
$520k
System design debt, quality issues
Total Skill Cost
$3.27M
Annual · FY24
Skill Gap Cost Composition
$3.27M across 3 categories
Cost to Close vs Cost to Ignore — Per Skill Cluster
ROI reframe
Skill Gap Cost Detail
Cost category · driver · FY24 $ · primary skill gap · closure cost · ROI
Cost CategoryDriverFY24 $Primary Skill GapClosure CostROI
Revenue DelayGen AI module slip + enterprise pipeline$1,850kLLM, MLOps, Ent Negotiation$440k4.2x
Contractor PremiumScarce infra skills sourced externally$920kK8s, AWS, SRE$180k5.1x
Delivery ReworkDesign gaps → technical debt cycles$520kSystem Design, Python Adv$55k9.5x
TOTAL SKILL-ATTRIBUTABLE COST$3,270k$760k closure cost (skills only)4.3x
The skills-only ROI argument: closing the 7 critical skill gaps costs $760k and avoids $3.27M of annual cost. That is a 4.3x return and a payback period of under 3 quarters. This is the skills-only portion — the combined picture (skills + competencies) totals $4.8M cost and $1.06M closure. See the Competency Gap Analysis page for the additional $1.51M in leadership, coordination, and IPO narrative cost.
AI Insight — Business Impact
The business impact section converts the skill analysis from an HR deliverable into a finance conversation. NovaTech spends $3.27M per year to work around skill gaps that cost $760k to close — a 4.3x return. The largest single line is revenue delay at $1.85M, driven almost entirely by the Gen AI module slip (LLM and MLOps gaps make the Q2 GA date infeasible) and enterprise deal delays (Enterprise Negotiation gaps slow the sales cycle). The highest-ROI single intervention is the $55k investment to close the System Design gap through an internal architecture review series — this avoids $520k of delivery rework at a 9.5x return. The contractor premium line ($920k) is the most structurally telling: the org is already paying to close some skill gaps, but it is paying 5x what it would cost to build the skills internally because contracting is reactive and training is preventive. Converting even half of the contractor spend to internal capability building would save $460k per year permanently. The skills-only $3.27M is 68% of the combined $4.8M capability cost. The remaining 32% ($1.51M) comes from competency gaps (leadership, coordination, IPO narrative) covered in the Competency Gap Analysis page.
Skill Gap Analysis Complete — 9 Sections · 58 Skills · 50 Employees · 5 Locations · 4 Tenure Bands · $3.27M Impact
Competency Gap Analysis
25 competencies · 5 categories · 13 roles · 10 departments · 50 employees · individual gaps · FY2024
Level 3 CSI 0.82 14 / 25 below benchmark
1 · Executive Summary — The Competency Picture in 60 Seconds
Org CSI

Benchmark 0.88 · Target 0.95
Below Benchmark

Top 4 Critical Competency Gaps — What Leadership Must Know
The 4 competencies blocking FY25 pipeline
#CompetencyCategoryGapHCBlocked OutcomeClosure PathAnnual Cost
1Strategic ThinkingLeadership0.288L5-L6 promotion pipelineBUILD · Exec coaching$450k
2Technical DepthTechnical0.2615Eng leadership credibilityBUILD · Mentorship$320k
3Hypothesis TestingCognitive0.2413Product decision qualityBUILD · Workshops$240k
4Systems ThinkingCognitive0.2211Cross-functional coordinationBUILD · Coaching$380k
AI Insight — Executive Summary
Competency gaps are different from skill gaps in two important ways. First, competencies do not show up on a resume or in a code review — they are inferred from behavior over time, which makes them harder to detect early and slower to close. Second, the cost of competency gaps lands in different categories than the cost of skill gaps: not in shipped product but in attrition, in coordination friction, and in the quality of executive narrative during a critical IPO window. NovaTech's CSI of 0.82 sits 6 points below the peer benchmark of 0.88 and 13 points below the internal target of 0.95. Four competencies (Strategic Thinking, Technical Depth, Hypothesis Testing, Systems Thinking) are simultaneously below the action threshold AND tied to the L5-L6 leadership pipeline that the company needs to grow into during FY25. The competency closure plan costs $300k against $1.51M of avoided cost — a 5.0x return that is actually higher than the 4.3x skill-side ROI because competency interventions (coaching, mentorship) cost less per outcome than technical training programs.
2 · Business Context — Why Competency Gaps Matter Right Now
FY25 Outcomes Blocked by Competency Gaps
Strategic moments that depend on competencies, not just skills
FY25 OutcomeTimingCompetencies RequiredCurrent StateRisk Level
L4→L5 promotion pipelineQ1-Q4 ongoingStrategic Thinking, Leadership, Coaching3 of 5 below benchmarkSevere
IPO board readinessQ4 FY25Executive Presence, Strategic Thinking, CommOrg-level deficitSevere
Cross-functional product launchesQ2-Q3Systems Thinking, Collaboration, InfluenceCoordination tax visibleSevere
Engineering leadership credibilityContinuousTechnical Depth, Coaching, Decision-MakingMid-mgr level weakSevere
Customer empathy at scaleContinuousCustomer Focus, Active ListeningAbove benchmarkHealthy
Talent retention (1-3yr cohort)Q1-Q4Coaching, Feedback, Manager EffectivenessManager layer weakHigh
Crisis decision qualityAs neededDecision-Making, Adaptability, ResilienceSenior-level OK, mid-mgr weakModerate
Why now: 4 of 7 strategic FY25 outcomes have severe competency-related risk. The IPO timeline in particular depends on board-level executive presence and strategic communication competencies that are not yet at the proficiency the public-company narrative requires. This analysis was triggered by the realization that the FY25 leadership pipeline is not competency-feasible at current state.
AI Insight — Business Context
The competency gap context is different from skills in one important way: competency gaps tend to surface during transitions, not during steady-state operations. Strategic Thinking and Executive Presence look fine when the company is operating under known conditions and become exposed when the company moves into a new state — IPO process, EMEA expansion, board scrutiny. NovaTech is heading into multiple such transitions simultaneously. The 4 critical competencies are not "weak today" — they are "insufficient for the company's next state." This is why they appear small in absolute terms (gaps of 0.22 to 0.28 on a 1.0 scale) but produce $1.51M in business cost: the cost lands when the new state arrives and the competencies are tested, not in the period they were measured.
3 · Scope of Analysis
Coverage
10 departments · 13 roles · 50 employees · 5 offices (SF, Bangalore, Austin, NYC, London) · FY2024
Competency Inventory
25 competencies across 5 categories: Cognitive (5), Technical (5), Leadership (6), Behavioral (5), Functional (4)
Assessment Methods
Self-assessment · Manager evaluation · Peer 360° · Behavioral interviews · Outcome observation · Higher reliance on multi-source than skills (VCS: 67%)
4 · Competency Framework — Measurement System
CSI Formula & Competency Categories
How competency gaps are measured
5 Competency Categories:
CognitiveStrategic Thinking, Systems Thinking, Hypothesis Testing, Decision-Making, Adaptability (5) TechnicalTechnical Depth, Architecture Sense, Code Quality, Debugging, Tools Mastery (5) LeadershipCoaching, Influence, Vision Setting, Executive Presence, Talent Development, Conflict Resolution (6) BehavioralActive Listening, Collaboration, Resilience, Empathy, Integrity (5) FunctionalCustomer Focus, Communication, Time Management, Goal Orientation (4)
CSI Formula:
CSI = Org Competency Score / Industry Benchmark

Scale: 0.0 - 1.0+
Benchmark: 0.88 (peer median)
Target: 0.95 (NovaTech internal target)
Current: CSI 0.82 (7% below benchmark, 14% below target)
Higher VCS (67%) than skills because competency assessment uses peer 360° as standard practice. Behavioral evidence is harder to fake than self-reported skill proficiency.
25 Competencies by Category
5 categories · count per category
Competencies by Criticality Tag
Critical · High · Medium · Low
AI Insight — Competency Framework
The framework's most important structural feature is the weighting of Leadership (6 competencies, 24% of inventory) and Cognitive (5 competencies, 20%). This is intentional and correct: as the organization scales toward IPO, both categories carry disproportionate business impact relative to their headcount. 17 of 25 competencies are tagged business-critical, but only 9 are fully covered at benchmark — meaning 47% of business-critical competencies are below the proficiency bar the org set for itself. The CSI methodology benchmarks against peer median (0.88) rather than internal aspiration (0.95), which gives the gap measurement external credibility for board reporting and investor due diligence. The 67% VCS on competency data (vs 54% on skills) is because peer 360° is standard practice for competency assessment but not for skill assessment — competency data is genuinely more triangulated.

Category Radar — Org vs Benchmark vs Target
5 competency categories

AI Insight — Current Competencies (AS-IS)
The 25-competency table reveals a striking pattern: NovaTech is structurally strong on Behavioral and Functional categories (8 of 9 competencies at or above benchmark) and structurally weak on Cognitive and Leadership categories (7 of 11 below benchmark). This is the cultural fingerprint of a company that hires for hustle, integrity, and customer empathy — and gets exactly that — but underweights the cognitive and leadership development that becomes essential at scale. Customer Focus at 0.92, Empathy at 0.92, Integrity at 0.95 — all well above benchmark — show what NovaTech is genuinely good at. Strategic Thinking at 0.60, Executive Presence at 0.65, Hypothesis Testing at 0.64 — all well below benchmark — show what the company is about to need and does not yet have. The good news: competency interventions (coaching, mentorship, structured stretch) are typically more cost-effective per outcome than skill training, which is why the closure ROI is higher (5.0x vs 4.3x).

AI Insight — Required Competencies (TO-BE)
The TO-BE picture shows the same rising bar pattern as skills: required CSI rises from 0.78 at L2 to 0.95 at L6, and the gap-to-current widens at every level. The forecasted demand line is the more important signal — it shows Leadership category demand rising from 0.85 (current bar) to 0.92 (FY25 end) to 0.95 (IPO readiness) over the next 6 quarters. This is not driven by an arbitrary aspiration; it is driven by the public-company narrative requirement, where executive presence, strategic communication, and board-level credibility become observable to external stakeholders. The L5-L6 layer carries the burden of this rising bar because that is the layer that interfaces with the board, investors, and analysts. Closing a 0.65 → 0.85 Executive Presence gap on 5 L5-L6 leaders is more business-critical than closing the same gap on 20 L3 individual contributors, even though the per-person investment is higher.

Category Contribution to Total Competency Gap
Where the gap concentrates by category
AI Insight — Competency Gaps (7A)
The master gap table shows that Leadership and Cognitive categories together account for 67% of total weighted competency gap (10 of 14 below-benchmark items). Technical category holds 3 of 14, including the single-largest weighted gap (Technical Depth at 3.90 — affects all 15 engineers). Functional category holds only 1 of 14 (Communication, low gap). Behavioral category contributes 0 below-benchmark items — every behavioral competency is at or above benchmark. The pattern is interpretable: NovaTech has built a culturally sound organization (strong Behavioral and Functional) but has not yet built the cognitive and leadership infrastructure that turns culture into scaled execution. The 4 critical items (Technical Depth, Hypothesis Testing, Systems Thinking, Strategic Thinking) all sit in the Cognitive + Leadership + Technical zone — exactly where the company must invest as it moves into the IPO and scale phase.

AI Insight — Department Competency Gap (7B)
Three departments carry all 4 critical competency gaps: Engineering (3 critical), Sales (2 critical), and Data/ML (1 critical) — the same three that carry the critical skill gaps. This is not coincidence. The roles that need critical skills are the same roles that need critical leadership and cognitive competencies, because both gaps are downstream of the same mechanism (rapid hiring + insufficient development infrastructure). Engineering carries the largest absolute weighted gap (4.20) because of its 18-person headcount combined with the Technical Depth deficit affecting all of them. Sales carries the highest leadership-specific concentration. The pattern means that every program targeting these three departments should pair a skill investment with a competency investment — they are the same audience, and integrated programs cost less per outcome than parallel ones.

AI Insight — Role Competency Gap (7C)
The role-level competency view shows the same capability inversion as skills: the two roles with the largest competency gaps (Director of Engineering 0.24, VP Sales 0.22) are also the two roles where competency matters most for the IPO trajectory. These two individuals will face the highest-stakes competency tests in FY25 (board interactions, investor roadshow prep, public-company narrative) and they enter that period below benchmark on Strategic Thinking and Executive Presence — the exact two competencies that determine credibility in those settings. The Engineering Manager layer (gap 0.18) is a different but equally important problem: this is the layer that owns coaching the next generation of leaders, but they themselves are below benchmark on Coaching, Talent Development, and Technical Depth. Without intervention, the competency deficit cascades downward to the people they manage.

AI Insight — Level Competency Gap (7D)
The competency gap by level shows the same monotonic structure as skills, just at smaller magnitude: competency gap grows from L2 (+0.07) to L6 (+0.24), adding +0.04 per level. The structural mechanism is identical — promotions outrun development — but the implications are different. Skill gaps are visible quickly (a missed PR review, a failed deployment) and create accountability pressure. Competency gaps are visible slowly (a misjudged board meeting, a weak coaching conversation) and accumulate without immediate accountability signals. This is why organizations chronically under-invest in competency development relative to skill development, and why NovaTech's L5-L6 layer carries gaps that are harder to detect but more strategically dangerous than the skill gaps in the same layer. The fix is structural (promotion gate that includes competency demonstration) plus targeted (executive coaching for the 5 individuals at L5-L6).

AI Insight — Individual Competency Gaps (7E)
The individual competency view reveals an important pattern: the top 20 employees with competency gaps overlap heavily with the top 20 employees with skill gaps. 16 of the same 20 employees appear on both lists. This is operationally useful because it means the intervention population is shared — the same 12-15 individuals in Engineering and Sales at L4-L5 are the people who need both skill programs (P01, P02, P03, P05, P06) AND competency interventions (P04 Leadership, P07 Hypothesis Testing, exec coaching). Integrated programs targeting this shared population cost less per person than running parallel skill and competency programs. The 4 employees who appear on the competency list but not the skill list (E024, E025, E017, E020) are mostly Product Managers and senior engineers whose technical skills are adequate but whose decision-making, hypothesis testing, and influence skills need development for next-level readiness.
7F · Competency Gap by Location
CSI by Office
5 offices · ranked by competency gap
Office × Competency Category Heatmap
Cognitive · Technical · Leadership · Behavioral · Functional
Office Competency Scorecard — All 5 Locations
HC · CSI · dominant cultural pattern · category strength
OfficeHCCSIStrongest CategoryWeakest CategoryCultural PatternStatus
London30.68FunctionalLeadershipIsolated · no leadership layer presentCritical · isolation
SF HQ220.76Behavioral, Customer FocusCognitive, LeadershipExec + product layer · leadership gap concentrated hereCritical · leadership weight
NYC50.85Behavioral, EmpathyStrategic ThinkingCustomer-facing strong · strategic layer thinAt Risk
Austin60.78Technical (Debug, Tools)Leadership, CoachingExecution strong · no middle management layerAt Risk
Bangalore140.82Technical (Tools, Debug)Strategic Thinking, InfluenceExecution-heavy · limited strategic autonomyAt Risk
The geographic competency pattern is different from the skill pattern: competencies cluster by office culture, not by office function. SF concentrates the leadership gap because the L5-L6 layer is co-located there. Austin concentrates execution strength but lacks coaching capability because it was hired for IC roles not management. Bangalore has strong Technical competencies but thin Strategic Thinking because it was scoped as an execution hub. London has no leadership layer at all because it is only 3 people.
AI Insight — Competency Gap by Location (7F)
The geographic competency view reveals what the skill view cannot: NovaTech has accidentally created a geographic competency monoculture in each office. SF carries the leadership gap because that's where all the senior roles are. Bangalore is strong on technical execution and weak on strategic autonomy because the office was scoped that way from founding. Austin was hired for pure IC roles so has no coaching competency layer. NYC is customer-facing so it's strong on empathy and weak on strategic thinking. London is too small (3 people) to have meaningful competency coverage in any category. The strategic implication is significant: when the company opens the EMEA office or grows the London presence, the competency profile of those locations will reflect the role scoping decisions made at hiring time — and if the current pattern is repeated (hire for execution, hope leadership follows), the same geographic competency gaps will reappear in the new locations within 2-3 years. The fix is in the role scoping, not the hiring pipeline. Recommended action: ensure the FY25 London hiring plan includes at least one L5-L6 role with explicit strategic responsibility, so the London office develops a leadership competency layer from the start rather than trying to retrofit one later.
7G · Competency Gap by Tenure Band
Competency Gap by Tenure Band
4 bands · competencies develop differently than skills
Competency Development Over Tenure
Leadership category trajectory
Tenure Band Competency Scorecard
All 4 bands · diagnosis · action lever
Tenure BandHC% of OrgComp GapDiagnosisAction Lever
0-1 year (new hires)1428%0.11Close to benchmark — hired with values + behavioral fit, cognitive development hasn't started yetMentorship + cohort-based development
1-3 years (growth cohort)1938%0.13Middle-of-pack — organically developed behavioral and functional competencies, cognitive/leadership plateauingStretch + coaching programs
3-5 years (established)1224%0.17Plateau risk — competency development slowed as role stabilized, leadership behaviors not developedL4-L5 promotion development (P04)
5+ years (veterans)510%0.21Competency drift — strategic thinking bar rose with company, veteran competencies not updatedExec coaching + re-contracting
AI Insight — Competency Gap by Tenure (7G)
The competency tenure pattern is monotonically increasing, not U-shaped like skills. This is one of the most important diagnostic differences between skills and competencies in the entire analysis. Skills show a U-curve because new hires arrive below-target (onboarding debt), improve in the 1-3 year window as the curriculum lands, and then drift again at 5+ years as roles evolve faster than individual learning. Competencies don't U-curve — they plateau. New hires arrive with the behavioral and functional competencies they were hired for, then cognitive and leadership development stalls because there is no formal infrastructure to continue it beyond onboarding. By year 5, the gap is 3x the new-hire gap. The pattern is diagnostic: it reveals that NovaTech has no ongoing competency development infrastructure at all — every employee's competency trajectory flattens after their first year because nothing in the environment continues to develop them. This is why the Leadership Development Cohort (P04) and executive coaching investments are so critical: they are the first formal competency development infrastructure the company will have. Without them, the monotonic curve continues and every additional year of tenure adds to the competency debt rather than reducing it.
7H · Critical Competency Gaps — Cross-Dimensional View (Dept · Role · Level · Location)

AI Insight — Critical Competency Gaps (7F)
The cross-dimensional critical competency view shows a different pattern from skills: critical competency gaps are broader (more departments, more roles, more levels) but smaller per-instance. Where critical skill gaps cluster narrowly in Engineering at L4-L5, critical competency gaps span Engineering, Sales, Product, and Data/ML across L3-L6. This breadth means competency programs need to be cohort-based rather than individually-targeted: P04 Leadership Cohort works because it brings 11 individuals across multiple departments together, and the cross-functional interaction itself is part of the development. The bus factor for competencies is meaningfully better than for skills (none at zero), which reflects the fact that competencies are organizational properties more than individual properties — losing one Strategic Thinker doesn't collapse the org's strategic thinking capability the way losing the K8s expert collapses the K8s capability.
8 · Competency Gap Insights — Patterns, Root Causes, Risks
PATTERN 1 · Cognitive-Leadership Concentration
10 of 14 below-benchmark competencies are in Cognitive (3) + Leadership (6) + 1 Technical Depth. Behavioral and Functional categories are at or above benchmark across the board. The deficit is in the cognitive-leadership infrastructure that turns culture into scaled execution.
Root cause: Hiring optimized for cultural fit and execution; cognitive and leadership development assumed to follow naturally with tenure (it did not).
PATTERN 2 · Slow-Visibility Compounding
Comp gap grows +0.04 per level (smaller than skill +0.43, but slower to detect). Gaps surface during transitions, not steady state. The IPO transition is the test that exposes the deficit on Strategic Thinking and Executive Presence at L5-L6.
Root cause: No competency-gated promotion criteria. Manager development not part of promotion conversation. Coaching capability not measured.
PATTERN 3 · Manager-Layer Bottleneck
Engineering Managers are below benchmark on Coaching (0.70 vs 0.85) and Talent Development (0.72 vs 0.85). They are simultaneously the layer that should develop the next generation AND the layer that needs development themselves. The cascade goes downward.
Root cause: Promoted to manager based on individual contributor performance; no formal manager development pathway before role; coaching is not rewarded or measured.
PATTERN 4 · Cultural Asymmetry
All 9 Behavioral + Functional competencies are at or above benchmark. Customer Focus 0.92, Empathy 0.92, Integrity 0.95. The org has built strong cultural foundations but has not built the analytical and leadership scaffolding on top. This is a real strength to preserve — interventions must not disrupt it.
Root cause: Founder-led hiring emphasized values and customer empathy; this worked well at startup scale and now needs to be augmented (not replaced) at scale-up scale.
AI Insight — Competency Gap Insights
The four patterns together reveal NovaTech's competency profile as a culturally strong but cognitively under-built organization. The Behavioral and Functional foundations are real and should be protected — they are the source of the company's customer empathy and execution discipline. The deficit is in the Cognitive-Leadership zone where the company needs to grow into its next state. The slow-visibility nature of competency gaps makes them especially dangerous in a pre-IPO window because the test of whether they have been closed will arrive in the form of board interactions, investor conversations, and public-company narrative — moments where there is no opportunity for course correction. Unlike skill gaps where contractor support can patch a deficit temporarily, competency gaps cannot be contracted around. Either the executive layer demonstrates strategic thinking at the moment of test, or it does not. This is why competency intervention is preventive medicine rather than reactive — and why the $300k investment must land before the IPO window opens, not during it.
9 · Business Impact — What Competency Gaps Cost in Revenue and Risk

Mid-level talent leaving under weak leadership
Coordination Tax

IPO Narrative Risk

Competency Cost Composition
$1.51M across 3 categories
Cost to Close vs Cost to Ignore — Per Competency Cluster
ROI reframe
Competency Gap Cost Detail
Cost category · driver · FY24 $ · primary comp gap · closure cost · ROI
Cost CategoryDriverFY24 $Primary Comp GapClosure CostROI
Attrition + Backfill1-3yr cohort exits under weak manager coaching$680kCoaching, Talent Dev, Tech Depth$120k5.7x
Coordination TaxCross-functional friction, slow decisions, rework$380kSystems Thinking, Influence$80k4.8x
IPO Narrative RiskExtra investor due diligence cycles, weaker pricing$450kStrategic Think, Exec Presence$100k4.5x
TOTAL COMP-ATTRIBUTABLE COST$1,510k$300k closure cost (comps only)5.0x
The competency-only ROI argument: closing the 4 critical competency gaps costs $300k and avoids $1.51M of annual cost. That is a 5.0x return — higher than the skills-side 4.3x because competency interventions (coaching, mentorship) cost less per outcome than technical training programs. Combined with the skills side, the total $1.06M closure plan addresses $4.8M of annual capability cost at 4.5x blended ROI. See the Skill Gap Analysis page for the $3.27M skills portion.
AI Insight — Business Impact
The competency business impact is structurally different from the skill business impact in three ways. First, the largest cost line ($680k attrition) is preventable with the smallest investment ($120k in coaching and manager development) — the highest-ROI single intervention in the entire $1.06M plan. The 1-3 year cohort attrition is driven primarily by weak manager coaching, and improving Coaching and Talent Development competencies in the 8-person manager layer would reduce that cohort's regrettable exit rate by an estimated 40%. Second, the IPO narrative risk ($450k) is the only category in the entire $4.8M cost framework that exists outside of normal operations — it is a transition cost, not a steady-state cost. This means it can be timed: if competency interventions land before the investor roadshow window opens, the cost is avoided entirely. If they land during the window, much of the cost is already incurred. Third, the coordination tax ($380k) is the most underestimated line because it is invisible — it shows up as slower product launches, longer decision cycles, more meetings, more rework. Closing Systems Thinking competency in the manager and senior IC layer would compound through every subsequent product cycle. The combined $4.8M / $1.06M / 4.5x story spans both pages: $3.27M skills cost + $1.51M competency cost = $4.8M total capability cost, addressed by $760k skill closure + $300k competency closure = $1.06M total closure plan.

Evidence quality
Methodology — How This TNA Was Conducted
Data collection · instruments · sample · evidence chain
Step 1 · Signal Collection
58 skills · 25 competencies · 50 employees · 13 roles · assessed across 5 evidence sources (self, manager, peer, test, outcome)
Step 2 · Gap Analysis
16 analytical cuts · Role-level Capability Gap Score (RCGS) +1.24, Competency Signal Index (CSI) 0.82 · 41 below-target items identified
Step 3 · Criticality Filter
41 → 11 critical · tagged by initiative block + business impact + velocity deficit
Step 4 · Root Cause Classification
Each gap classified: skill deficit · process flaw · tool/tech gap · behavioral issue · structural cause
Step 5 · Role-Specific View
Same findings · narrated through each role's decision rights + KPIs + language
📋 Data Collection Methods
Structured competency survey (50 employees · self-assessment)
Manager assessment forms (13 reporting managers)
Peer calibration sessions (cross-functional panels · 6 sessions)
Skills proficiency tests (technical roles · 28 employees)
Performance outcome review (FY24 delivery data)
1:1 diagnostic interviews (leadership layer · 8 interviews)
👥 Sample Coverage
Total population: 50 employees (100%)
Roles assessed: 13 of 13
Skill domains: 58 skills across 11 domains
Competency domains: 25 competencies across 5 clusters
Levels covered: L2 through L6
Locations: SF · Bangalore · Austin · NYC · London
🛠️ Tools Used
Skills Intelligence Platform (SIP) — primary assessment engine
Custom competency rubrics (5-level proficiency scale)
RCGS formula (Role-level Capability Gap Score)
CSI index (Competency Signal Index · 0–1 scale)
Skills Velocity Tracker (quarterly delta measurement)
Workforce Risk Heatmap (bus factor + flight risk matrix)
🎯 Desired Performance State (TO-BE) — Year-End FY25 Targets
≤ 0.65
Role-level Capability Gap Score (RCGS) target
from RCGS +1.24 · 48% reduction

4 Critical Competency Gaps — Current vs Required Proficiency
Competency Signal Index (CSI) per competency · peer benchmark 0.88
Why competency gaps differ from skill gaps: Competencies are inferred from behaviour over time, not demonstrated in a task or test. They are slower to develop, harder to measure, and more expensive to close. The 4 critical competency gaps cost $1.51M per year in avoidable business impact — more than the skill-gap cost per item — because their effects land in attrition, coordination friction, and leadership pipeline failure, not in shipped-product delays.
Skill vs Competency Gap Classification — All 11 Critical Gaps
Explicit labelling of gap type · determines intervention approach · not interchangeable
Gap Capability Gap Type Cluster Roles Affected Proficiency Level Required Current Proficiency Gap Score Why It Matters
G01 Large Language Model (LLM) / Gen AI Proficiency Skill gap Technical Machine Learning Engineer · Staff Engineer L4 — Advanced Application L1–L2 — Awareness only +2.40 Blocks Gen AI module delivery in Q2
G02 Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) / Infrastructure Skill gap Technical Machine Learning Engineer · DevOps Engineer L4 — Advanced Application L1 — None (Bus Factor of 0 — no internal coverage) +2.60 Single point of failure — no internal coverage
G03 Kubernetes (K8s) / Container Orchestration Skill gap Technical DevOps · Engineering Manager · Senior Engineer L4 — Proficient L2–L3 — Basic (Bus Factor of 1 — single expert dependency) +2.10 Blocks cloud migration; single expert dependency
G04 Cloud Architecture (Amazon Web Services + Site Reliability Engineering) Skill gap Technical Senior Engineer · Staff Engineer · Engineering Manager L4 — Proficient L2–L3 — Basic +1.80 Delivery reliability risk across cloud-dependent product
G05 Strategic Thinking — Leadership Application Competency gap Cognitive + Leadership Director of Engineering · VP Sales · Engineering Managers L5 — Expert (Competency Signal Index 0.88) Competency Signal Index 0.60 — Critical +2.84 Root cause of $680k mid-level attrition
G06 Enterprise Negotiation Skill gap Functional VP Sales · Senior Account Executive · Customer Success Manager L5 — Expert L3 — Developing (Bus Factor of 1 — single expert dependency) +2.66 Deal loss and cycle elongation in Enterprise pipeline
G07 Technical Depth Competency gap Technical competency Engineering Manager · Senior Engineer · Staff Engineer L4 — Proficient (Competency Signal Index 0.88) Competency Signal Index 0.62 — Critical +1.65 Engineering credibility and decision quality at L4–L5
G08 Hypothesis Testing / Data Literacy Competency gap Cognitive competency Product Manager · Engineering Manager · Senior Account Executive L4 — Proficient (Competency Signal Index 0.88) Competency Signal Index 0.64 — Below threshold +1.40 Product decision quality and A/B testing rigour
G09 System Design Skill gap Technical Senior Engineer · Staff Engineer · Machine Learning Engineer L4 — Proficient L2–L3 — Basic +1.55 Architecture debt and platform scalability risk
G10 Strategic Account Management Skill + Competency Functional + Behavioural Customer Success Manager · Senior Account Executive · Account Executive L4 — Proficient L2–L3 — Developing +1.30 Net Revenue Retention (NRR) impact and customer expansion
G11 Onboarding Curriculum (0–1 Year Cohort) Process gap Functional + All clusters All new hires across all functions (0–1 year tenure) L2–L3 by end of ramp L1–L2 — Ramp debt from obsolete curriculum +1.20 Delayed time-to-productivity for all new hires
SUMMARY 7 Skill gaps · 3 Competency gaps · 1 Process gap Competency gaps (G05, G07, G08) require coached behavioural change — not skill training. Treating them as skill gaps is the single most common cause of L&D investment waste.

Every chart is framed around what the gap is costing and which FY25 commitments are at risk. Intervention design belongs to the next stage (TNI). The question this view answers: "What is the current capability position of this company, what is it costing us, and which FY25 commitments are at risk if it is not addressed?"

Business Impact — The $4.8M Annual Cost of the Capability Gap
Gap economics · 6 cost categories · FY24 actual
The diagnostic finding: NovaTech's 11 critical capability gaps are generating $4.8M per fiscal year in quantifiable business cost across 6 categories — revenue delay, contractor premium, attrition and backfill, delivery rework, coordination tax, and initiative infeasibility. The gap is concentrated in Engineering and Sales, in the L5–L6 leadership layer, and across 7 critical skill domains. This cost is ongoing and compounds each quarter the gaps remain unaddressed.
Where the $4.8M is Leaking
6 cost categories · FY24

Upper-Right — High criticality, high coverage · on track
