TOTAL ANNUAL COST — 11 CRITICAL GAPS $4,780k / year · compounding each quarter unaddressed
The Uncomfortable Roadmap Conversation
4 of 7 FY25 initiatives are not capability-feasible at current velocity
The honest executive question: at current closure velocity, 0 of 11 critical gaps close before their business-required date. Four FY25 initiatives are delivery-dependent on gaps that will not close in time even with the proposed $1.06M plan. These four items should be re-timed in the FY25 plan now, not discovered as slips at launch. The capability-adjusted roadmap is a C-Suite decision, not an engineering escalation.
Board-Ready Narrative — The 300-Word Version
Copy/paste into the capability update slide

Capability position — headline. NovaTech is operating at approximately 64% of the role-required capability defined by its own job architecture. The gap is concentrated in two functions (Engineering and Sales) and in the senior leadership layer (L5–L6), where capability is furthest from the business impact those roles carry. Eleven capabilities are currently blocking FY25 roadmap commitments.

Financial impact of the gap. The 11 critical capability gaps are costing NovaTech approximately $4.8M per fiscal year across revenue delay, contractor premium, attrition and backfill, delivery rework, and coordination tax. The largest single line is revenue delay from the Gen AI module and Enterprise pipeline, at $1.85M. The leadership gap is generating $680k in annual mid-level attrition and is traceable to a structural flaw in the promotion mechanic, not individual performance.

Root cause summary. Nine of 11 gaps are genuine skill deficits addressable through capability-building interventions. Two gaps — the L5–L6 leadership gap and the 0–1 year onboarding debt — are structural problems that require process redesign before any learning intervention can produce durable results. Attempting to train around these two without fixing their structural causes has been the pattern in prior years and explains why the gaps have compounded rather than closed.

Roadmap-feasibility diagnosis. Four FY25 initiatives (Gen AI General Availability (GA), Cloud Migration Phase 2, Enterprise Go-to-Market (GTM), Global Expansion in Europe, Middle East and Africa (EMEA)) are not capability-feasible at current closure velocity. These initiatives are delivery-dependent on gaps G01, G02, G03, and G06 respectively — none of which will close before the initiative launch dates without deliberate intervention. Acknowledging this now enables informed re-timing; discovering it at launch produces sunk cost and credibility damage.

The diagnostic conclusion: the 11 critical gaps identified through this TNA represent a $4.8M annual drag that is structural, concentrated, and quantifiable. The gap is not distributed evenly — 80% of the cost sits in 4 gaps (G01, G02, G05, G06), and those 4 gaps map to 3 of the 7 highest-priority FY25 commitments. This analysis provides the evidential basis for the next stage: Training Needs Identification.

AI Insight — C-Suite View
The most important diagnostic finding in this view is the concentration pattern: 80% of the $4.8M annual gap cost sits in four gaps (G01 Large Language Model (LLM) proficiency, G02 MLOps, G05 Strategic Leadership, G06 Enterprise Negotiation), and those four gaps map directly to the three most important FY25 revenue commitments. This is not a distributed capability problem — it is a concentrated one with a clear population target. The second critical finding is the structural misclassification: two of the 11 gaps (G05 and G11) look like training problems but are actually structural design problems. The leadership gap is a promotion mechanic problem, not a coaching gap. The onboarding debt is a curriculum obsolescence problem, not a delivery gap. This distinction matters enormously for the next stage (TNI) — designing learning programs for structural problems produces well-delivered interventions with zero behavior change at the 6-month mark. The roadmap-feasibility finding is the one most likely to be avoided: four FY25 initiatives are not deliverable at current capability velocity. This analysis makes that explicit. The executive conversation this enables — acknowledging the infeasibility now and re-timing deliberately — is uncomfortable but categorically cheaper than discovering the same fact at launch. The diagnostic conclusion: NovaTech has a $4.8M concentrated capability gap in 11 identifiable areas, 2 of which require structural fixes before any learning investment will hold. This analysis provides the evidential foundation for Training Needs Identification.
👥
CHRO View — Workforce Risk & Talent Stability
CHRO · VP People · Talent Strategy · Comp & Rewards
What this view optimizes for: workforce stability, attrition risk, retention levers, bus factor exposure, promotion pipeline health, and the structural mechanisms that produce capability gaps in the first place. Every chart here answers a workforce or people decision. Financial framing is secondary; people risk is primary. The question this view answers: "Which capability gaps are actually workforce stability problems, and what are the retention and development levers I can pull to close them?"
Attrition Cost at Risk
$680k
Mid-level leaving under weak leadership
Bus Factor ≤ 2
12 items
Single points of capability failure
1–3 Yr Retention Cohort
19 / 50
38% · healthiest cohort · protect
Promotion Mechanic
+0.43
Gap added per level · broken
Attrition Economics — What the Leadership Gap Is Costing in People Terms
$680k / yr attrition · $45k exec coaching closes it · 15.1x Return on Investment (ROI)
The CHRO reframe: The single highest-ROI investment in the entire $1.06M plan is $45k of executive coaching against the leadership competency gap. Not because coaching is magical, but because the leadership gap is the upstream cause of $680k in annual mid-level attrition. When leaders cannot develop their teams, mid-level talent leaves. The attrition is the symptom; the leadership gap is the disease. This is the one workforce intervention that pays back in under a quarter.
Bus Factor Exposure — Single Points of Failure
12 items at Bus Factor (BF) ≤ 2 · 3 already collapsed
Tenure Curve — U-Shaped Distribution
New hires + 5+ yr both carry highest gaps
Role × Workforce Risk Matrix
Flight risk · replacement cost · bus factor · combined severity
CHRO Action Levers — 7 FY25 Moves Filtered for People Decisions
Owned or co-owned by CHRO · workforce and structural
# Move People Outcome Owner / Co-Owner Retention Impact Structural Fix? ROI
2 Executive coaching program Closes leadership gap that drives mid-level attrition CHRO · CPO High · 5-7 retained No · skills 15.1x
5 L4→L5 promotion gate Fixes the mechanism producing +0.43 gap per promotion CHRO · CPO Medium · 3-4 retained Yes · structural 5.6x
6 London hiring stream (EMEA) Builds workforce in a location that currently has none CHRO · CRO n/a · greenfield No · hiring qualitative
1 ML + MLOps hiring Replaces capability absent in 50-person org CTO · CHRO sourcing n/a · hiring No · hiring 2.8x
4 Kubernetes (K8s) contractor + shadowing Protects against single-person bus factor risk VP Infra · CHRO retention Medium · bus-factor 1 protection Partial · knowledge transfer 4.0x
7 90-day revalidation sprint Produces auditable workforce data for board reporting CHRO · CoS n/a · measurement No · data quality qualitative
Retention Protection Plan — The 1-3 Year Cohort
19 people · 38% of org · healthiest capability · highest flight risk
The non-obvious CHRO insight: the 1-3 year cohort of 19 people carries the smallest capability gap (Role-level Capability Gap Score (RCGS) +1.25, well below the org average of +1.24) and represents 38% of the entire workforce. They are also the most flight-prone demographic in the tech labor market. Losing a 1-3 year employee to a competitor is functionally equivalent to losing 18 months of invested capability closure work at current velocity rates. Protecting retention on this cohort specifically — through stretch projects, visible ownership, accelerated advancement, and explicit career pathing — is the highest-yield people move available in FY25. It is also the move most often underfunded because the cohort is already performing well, which creates the illusion that they do not need investment.
The Promotion Mechanic — Why Gaps Grow With Every Level
Monotonic +0.43 per level · L2 → L6 · capability inversion
The structural finding: NovaTech's skill gap grows monotonically from L2 (+0.60) to L6 (+2.75), adding +0.43 of uncompensated gap at every promotion. The mechanism is that promotion velocity exceeds development velocity — people are advanced to the next level before they have closed the current level's expectations, and the debt compounds. This is not an individual performance issue; it is a design flaw in the promotion process. The fix is structural, not training-based: Move 5 in the plan is to install a promotion gate at L4→L5 (the point where the curve steepens) that requires documented closure of the previous level's capability expectations, not just performance review scores. No amount of downstream training will compensate for a broken upstream promotion mechanic.
CHRO Summary — People-First Read of the 16 Gap Cuts
Workforce version of the dashboard in 250 words

Workforce position. NovaTech has 50 employees carrying a capability gap concentrated at the leadership layer (L5 and L6), where promotion debt has compounded into a +2.75 skill gap at the Director level. Twelve capabilities sit at bus factor 2 or below, including 6 at bus factor 1 where a single resignation would collapse the function. Three capabilities are already at Bus Factor of 0 (zero internal coverage), meaning the organization has been delivering dependent work through workarounds for some time.

Retention picture. The org loses approximately $680k per year to mid-level attrition that is traceable to a leadership competency gap at L5–L6. This is the single largest people-cost line in the capability analysis and the one with the highest closure ROI at 15.1x. The healthiest cohort is the 1-3 year band (19 people, 38% of the org) which is also the most flight-prone demographic in tech labor markets.

Structural finding. Skill gaps grow monotonically from L2 to L6 at a rate of +0.43 per level. This is not individual performance drift; it is a broken promotion mechanic that carries forward unclosed expectations at every level transition. Fixing this at L4→L5 is a structural CHRO decision, not a training deliverable.

CHRO diagnostic conclusions for TNA: (1) the leadership gap (G05) is the highest-cost people finding — $680k annual attrition traceable to leadership competency deficit at L5–L6; (2) the L4→L5 promotion mechanic is a structural flaw requiring policy redesign — not a training problem; (3) the 1–3 year cohort of 19 people is the highest-priority retention risk and needs explicit development investment; (4) the ML and Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) capability absence (G01, G02) requires external hiring — internal development alone cannot close it at required velocity; (5) bus factor exposure across 12 items represents a workforce continuity risk that must be addressed as part of any capability closure approach.

AI Insight — CHRO View
The CHRO view reframes the capability gap as a workforce stability and retention problem, which is the frame most likely to unlock action at the people-decision layer. The single most important people finding is that 15.1x of the total ROI in the $1.06M plan is concentrated in one $45k line item — executive coaching for the leadership gap. This is cheap, fast, and addresses the upstream cause of the attrition bleed. It should not wait for Q2 budget cycles; it can start in 30 days using discretionary spend. The second most important finding is structural: the promotion mechanic at L4→L5 is producing gaps faster than training can close them, and no amount of L&D spend will fix that until the mechanism itself is addressed. This is the CHRO decision that most often gets delegated to L&D and should not be — it is an org design and promotion policy decision owned at the CHRO level. The third finding is the retention opportunity hiding in plain sight: the 1-3 year cohort is the healthiest and the most protectable, but it is also the cohort most likely to be skipped in retention planning because their performance already looks good. That is precisely why they need explicit protection — their current healthy state is the result of prior investment, and that investment is harvestable by competitors if not protected. Recommended CHRO sequencing: (1) approve executive coaching this week, (2) scope the L4→L5 promotion gate design for Q2 launch, (3) run a retention diagnostic on the 19-person 1-3 year cohort in the next 30 days, (4) formalize CHRO co-ownership on hiring moves 1 and 6 to prevent capability hiring from being pure CTO/CRO decisions without workforce input.
🎓
CLO View — Learning Requirements Characterization
Chief Learning Officer · Head of L&D · Curriculum Architects · Learning Experience Designers
What this view optimizes for: understanding the nature of each learning need — what type of learning it requires, how complex it is, who needs it, whether internal expertise exists to address it, and which gaps share learner populations. Program design, delivery modes, and curriculum specifications belong to the next stage (Training Needs Identification). The question this view answers: "What does each of the 11 critical gaps actually require in terms of learning type, population, and internal readiness — before we decide how to address it?"
Gaps Requiring Learning
9 / 11
2 require structural fix first
Gaps Needing External Expertise
4
Zero internal experts in these domains
Behavior Change (not just knowledge)
6 / 9
Require L3/L4 outcomes · harder to close
Learner Population
38 / 50
Employees with at least one learning need
Learning Type Classification — What Each Gap Actually Requires
9 learning-addressable gaps · knowledge vs skill vs behavior vs structural
Why this classification matters: knowledge gaps (G08, G10) can be addressed through short-format workshops and assessments — they are the fastest to close. Skill-build gaps (G03, G04, G07, G09) require deliberate practice and application cycles — 8–16 weeks minimum. Behavior change gaps (G05, G06) require coached application and feedback loops over months — the fastest-growing category and the most likely to fail if treated as knowledge transfer. Two gaps (G05 leadership, G11 onboarding) cannot be addressed through learning alone and require structural intervention first — see OD Head view.
Internal Expertise Availability
Can we teach this internally? · per gap
Learning Complexity Assessment
Effort tier per gap · weeks to close at adequate intensity
Learner Population Analysis — Who Needs What
11 gaps · role population · prerequisite · shared learner potential
Gap Capability Need Learner Population (roles) Headcount Learning Type Internal Expert Available? Structural Prerequisite? Gap Adjacency
G01 LLM / Gen AI Proficiency ML Engineer · Staff Eng · Sr Engineer 8 Skill-build No · Bus Factor (BF) of 0 No G02 (MLOps)
G02 MLOps / ML Infrastructure ML Engineer · DevOps · Staff Eng 6 Skill-build No · Bus Factor (BF) of 0 No G01 (LLM)
G03 Kubernetes / Container Orchestration DevOps · Eng Manager · Sr Engineer 13 Skill-build Partial · Bus Factor (BF) of 1 No G04 (Cloud)
G04 Cloud Architecture (Amazon Web Services (AWS) + Site Reliability Engineering (SRE)) Sr Eng · Staff Eng · Eng Manager 14 Skill-build Partial · Bus Factor (BF) of 2 No G03 (K8s)
G05 Strategic Thinking (Leadership) Dir Eng · VP Sales · Eng Managers 11 Behavior change Partial · coaching Yes — promotion gate G06 (negotiation)
G06 Enterprise Negotiation VP Sales · Senior Account Executive (AE) · CSM 9 Behavior change No · Bus Factor (BF) of 1 No G10 (strat accounts)
G07 Technical Depth Eng Manager · Sr Eng · Staff Eng 15 Skill-build Yes · L4+ mentors No G01, G09
G08 Hypothesis Testing / Data Literacy Product Mgr · Eng Manager · Senior Account Executive (AE) 13 Knowledge + skill Yes · data team No None
G09 System Design Sr Engineer · Staff Eng · ML Eng 9 Skill-build Partial · Staff Eng No G07 (tech depth)
G10 Strategic Account Management CSM · Senior Account Executive (AE) · Acct Exec 9 Knowledge + skill No · Bus Factor (BF) of 1 No G06 (negotiation)
G11 Onboarding Curriculum All new hires (0–1yr) 14+ Knowledge + process Yes · L&D team Yes — curriculum rewrite None
Gap Adjacency — Which Can Be Addressed Together
Shared learner population · consolidation opportunity
Consolidation insight: G01+G02 share the same Engineering learner population and have identical internal-expertise absence — these should be addressed in one combined intervention. G03+G04 share learner overlap and cloud-infrastructure context. G06+G10 share the Sales population. G05 is the most isolated — it needs its own approach because behavior change in leadership requires individual coaching, not cohort learning.
Internal Expert Depth by Gap Domain
Who can teach · L4+ availability · bus factor per domain
Expert scarcity flags: G01 (LLM) and G02 (MLOps) have zero internal experts at the required level — any learning intervention in these areas will require external facilitation. G06 (Enterprise Negotiation) and G10 (Strategic Accounts) are similarly undersupplied internally. These 4 gaps must be flagged as external-dependency needs in the Training Needs Identification (TNI) stage.
CLO Summary — Learning Requirements in 250 Words
The learning characterization read

Learning type distribution. Of the 11 critical gaps, 9 are addressable through learning interventions and 2 require structural fixes first (see OD Head view). Of the 9 learnable gaps: 4 are skill-build gaps requiring deliberate practice over 8–16 weeks; 2 are behavior change gaps requiring coached application over months; 3 are knowledge and skill gaps addressable through structured learning formats. This distribution is important — the behavior change gaps (G05 leadership, G06 negotiation) will dominate the timeline and require the most investment in outcome measurement.

Internal expertise reality. Four gaps (G01, G02, G06, G10) have zero or near-zero internal expert depth. Any learning design for these gaps will depend on external facilitation, which adds vendor sourcing, contracting, and quality-assurance requirements before the first learner session can be designed. This is the single most important constraint the CLO brings to the Training Needs Identification (TNI) conversation — it determines the build/buy/borrow split before a single program title is written.

Adjacency opportunities. The 9 learnable gaps consolidate into approximately 6–7 logical learning clusters based on shared populations and subject matter. G01+G02 share the ML Engineering population. G03+G04 share the cloud infrastructure population. G06+G10 share the Sales population. This consolidation insight reduces learner fragmentation and facilitator overload — it is the CLO's primary design input to Training Needs Identification (TNI).

CLO handoff to Training Needs Identification (TNI): 9 learning-addressable gaps · 4 requiring external expertise · 2 structural prerequisites from OD · 6–7 logical learning clusters by population adjacency · 38 employees in the learning population · behavior change gaps (G05, G06) require outcome-measurement design from the start, not retrofit.

AI Insight — CLO View
The CLO's most important contribution to the TNA stage is not program design — that belongs to Training Needs Identification (TNI) — it is honest classification of what kind of learning each gap actually requires. Most capability analyses collapse this step and jump directly to curriculum, which is why so many leadership development programs produce Kirkpatrick Level 1 smile sheets and zero behavior change at 6 months. The classification work done here prevents that. The critical finding is the behavior-change concentration: gaps G05 and G06 — the two highest-cost gaps in terms of attrition and deal impact — both require behavior change interventions, not knowledge transfer. Behavior change cannot be compressed into a 2-day workshop. It requires coached application, manager reinforcement, and feedback loops over months. Any TNI design that treats these as knowledge gaps will undersize the intervention and produce disappointing outcomes. The second finding is the external-dependency flag: four gaps (G01, G02, G06, G10) have no internal expert who can teach them. This means the Training Needs Identification (TNI) stage will inherit a vendor sourcing requirement for those four gaps before any program can be designed. Surfacing this now — in the TNA diagnostic — gives the L&D Ops function lead time on sourcing, which is typically the longest-lead item in the entire program development cycle. The CLO diagnostic conclusion: 9 learnable gaps, 4 requiring external expertise, 2 with structural prerequisites, 38 employees in scope, and 6–7 natural learning clusters by population adjacency. This characterization is the input that makes TNI tractable.
🧭
OD Head View — Structural Interventions & System Design
Head of Organization Development · Change Management · Culture & Design
What this view optimizes for: structural rather than behavioral interventions, distinguishing training-solvable gaps from design-flaw gaps, change readiness, culture signals, and the system-level mechanisms that produce the gaps in the first place. The question this view answers: "Which of these needs is actually a structure problem in disguise, which is a genuine learning need, and which structural interventions have to happen before any training investment can produce durable results?"
Structural Interventions
4
Of 7 total FY25 moves
Structure Problem Disguised as Training
3 / 11
Cannot be trained around
Change Readiness Score
62%
Org can absorb this much change
Culture Signal
Moderate
Mid-level bleed is the tell
Training vs Structure — Reclassifying the 11 Critical Gaps
Not every "training need" is solvable through training
The OD reframe: Of the 11 gaps classified as "training needs" in the default view, 3 are actually structural problems that cannot be closed through any training program. The L5-L6 leadership gap will not close through executive coaching alone because the underlying cause is a broken L4→L5 promotion mechanic. The Director of Engineering gap will not close through coursework because the role itself was scoped 2 years ago against a different product strategy. The 0-1 year onboarding debt will not close through better onboarding until the curriculum is rewritten against FY25 role definitions. Trying to train around any of these three is a 6-12 month waste of budget. The OD job is to identify them and get them fixed structurally before the training investment lands on unstable ground.
The 3 Intervention Layers — Where Each Gap Actually Lives
Structural · Design · Behavioral · sequencing matters
Sequencing rule: structural fixes must land before or in parallel with training interventions, never after. Training applied on top of an unfixed structure produces short-term learning that does not persist because the system metabolizes it back into the original state. This is why L&D programs aimed at "leadership development" at high-growth companies often show strong post-program evaluation scores but zero behavior change at the 6-month mark — the structure reclaimed the trained behavior. Fix the promotion gate first, then run the leadership program. Never the reverse.
4 Structural Interventions Required Before or Alongside Training
OD-owned work · non-training but gap-closing
# Structural Intervention Gap It Enables Type OD Owner Duration Training Depends On This?
S1 L4→L5 promotion gate redesign Leadership gap (Strategic Thinking, Tech Depth) Policy + process OD Head + CHRO 8 wks design · ongoing Yes — P04 Leadership Dev depends on this
S2 Role definition refresh for L5-L6 Director of Eng + VP Sales gaps Org design OD Head + CPO 6 wks Yes — need clean target before coaching
S3 Onboarding curriculum rewrite to FY25 roles 0-1 yr tenure debt Process + content OD Head + L&D 10 wks Partial — P09 replaces current curriculum
S4 Protected expert time allocation for L4+ internal mentors Enables P05 Tech Depth Mentorship Resource + policy OD Head + CTO 4 wks design · ongoing Yes — P05 impossible without this
Change Readiness Radar — Can the Org Absorb This?
6 dimensions · current state vs readiness threshold
Change absorption capacity: the 7-move FY25 plan is a meaningful change load for a 50-person organization. Change readiness across 6 dimensions averages 62%, below the 75% threshold typically required for a multi-move change portfolio. The two weakest dimensions are middle manager capacity (the managers who will execute the change are themselves in the gap, which is a bootstrapping problem) and change fatigue history (the org has run two prior capability programs in the last 18 months with mixed follow-through). The OD job is to stage the rollout rather than compress it — parallel execution of all 7 moves will exceed absorption capacity and produce completion rates below 50% across the portfolio.
Culture Signals Embedded in the Gap Data
What the numbers say about how this org actually works
SIGNAL 1 · Promotion velocity > development velocity
The monotonic gap growth from L2 to L6 says this organization rewards delivery over growth. People are promoted when they ship, not when they are ready for the next level. The reward system is reinforcing the wrong behavior, which is why additional training will not fix it without a policy change.
SIGNAL 2 · Single-point-of-failure tolerance
12 items at Bus Factor ≤ 2 indicates this org accepts key-person risk as a normal operating condition. This is a culture tell. Organizations that distribute expertise do so because they have a documented norm of pairing, shadowing, and knowledge transfer. NovaTech does not. Adding that norm is an OD intervention, not a training one.
SIGNAL 3 · 56% assessment confidence
Half of the capability assessment is single-source self-report. This indicates a culture of low measurement investment on people questions. Business metrics are tracked daily; capability is assessed annually. The gap in assessment rigor is itself a culture signal about what the org considers worth measuring.
SIGNAL 4 · Healthy 1-3 year cohort
The middle-tenure cohort shows the smallest gaps, which means the org is capable of developing people well during a specific window. This is a positive culture signal — the capacity exists. The question is why that capacity fades at L4+ transitions. The answer is the promotion mechanic, which is a fixable structural problem, not a culture change.
OD Head Summary — 250 Words on the Structure-Behavior Split
The systemic read

The OD framing. Of the 11 critical capability gaps, 3 are structure problems disguised as training problems and cannot be closed through curriculum. The L5-L6 leadership gap will recur indefinitely until the L4→L5 promotion mechanic is redesigned. The Director of Engineering and VP Sales gaps reflect role definitions that drifted 2 years ago and have not been formally updated. The 0-1 year onboarding debt persists because the onboarding curriculum is still teaching the FY23 job.

The sequencing rule. Structural interventions must land before or in parallel with training investments, never after. Training that lands on an unfixed structure produces short-term gains that the system metabolizes back within 6 months. This is why the 4 structural interventions identified in this view are not optional — they are prerequisites for the training catalog to produce durable outcomes.

The change readiness constraint. The 7-move FY25 plan is a meaningful change load for a 50-person organization with 62% change readiness. The single biggest absorption risk is that the middle managers who will execute the change are themselves in the capability gap, creating a bootstrapping problem. The plan needs to be staged rather than run in parallel.

OD diagnostic conclusions for TNA: (1) the L4→L5 promotion gate redesign (S1) is the highest-priority structural intervention — without it, any leadership development effort will recur as a remediation expense every 12–18 months; (2) L5–L6 role definitions must be refreshed (S2) before any behavior-change interventions for gaps G05 and G06 can be designed — coaching against stale role expectations produces the right behavior for the wrong job; (3) protected expert time policy (S4) is a prerequisite for using internal L4+ experts in technical learning interventions for gap G07; (4) the org's 62% change readiness score sets a hard ceiling on concurrent change load — this must be respected in TNI sequencing; (5) the culture signals identified (promotion velocity, bus-factor tolerance, low measurement investment) are systemic levers that training cannot fix alone.

AI Insight — OD Head View
The OD view is the one most likely to be ignored and the one most important to run first. The pattern in capability reports is that the CLO gets the learning requirements, the CHRO gets the attrition line, and the OD Head gets nothing actionable because structural findings are harder to monetize in quarterly reviews. The finding this view surfaces is that 3 of 11 critical gaps cannot be closed through any learning intervention — they are broken structures wearing training labels. The leadership gap is a promotion mechanic problem. The Director of Engineering gap is a role definition problem. The onboarding debt is a curriculum obsolescence problem. Running any learning intervention on these three without first fixing their structural causes produces short-term completion rates that look fine in the dashboard and zero behavior persistence at the 6-month mark — a pattern NovaTech has already experienced in prior years. The most important single OD finding in this TNA is the sequencing constraint: the 4 structural interventions (S1–S4) must complete or be in-flight before any learning intervention targeting the affected gaps can be designed in TNI, let alone delivered. This is not a scheduling preference — it is a hard prerequisite rule. Structural work is slow, political, and unglamorous. Learning work is fast, visible, and measurable. The institutional temptation is always to start the learning while the structural work is "in progress." That is how capability programs fail at 6 months. OD diagnostic conclusion: S1 and S2 are highest-priority structural interventions, required before any behavior-change intervention for gaps G05 and G06 can be TNI-designed. S3 must complete before any onboarding curriculum refresh can be delivered. S4 must be policy before internal expert facilitation for gap G07 can be confirmed. None are optional.
🤝
HRBP View — Named Individuals & Manager Conversations
HR Business Partner · Manager Enablement · IDP Coaching · Employee Experience
What this view optimizes for: named individuals, conversation scripts, Individual Development Plan templates, manager readiness, and the quarterly people conversation calendar. This view trades aggregation for specificity — the question is not "what is the org-wide gap" but "who do I call this week, and what do I say to them and their manager?" The question this view answers: "Which specific individuals need a development conversation in the next 30 days, what should that conversation cover, and what IDP outputs should come out of it?"
Individuals Needing Individual Development Plans (IDPs)
38
76% of org · priority-sequenced
Conversations This Q
17
Critical path · must happen
Manager Readiness
58%
Managers ready for dev talks
IDP Templates Available
5
One per conversation type
Priority Conversation Roster — Q1 FY25
17 individuals · role · gap · conversation type · Individual Development Plan (IDP) template · manager
# Employee (role) Primary Gap Conversation Type Manager IDP Template Timing
1 E001 · Director of Engineering Technical Depth + Strategic Thinking Executive coaching intake CEO T1 · Exec IDP Week 1
2 E007 · VP Sales Ent Negotiation + Strategic Thinking Executive coaching intake CEO T1 · Exec IDP Week 1
3 E014 · Sr DevOps Lead (Bangalore) Bus-factor protection (K8s/TF/Mesh) Retention + knowledge-transfer plan Eng Manager T2 · Expert retention Week 1
4 E003 · Engineering Manager A Leadership skills + Tech Depth Development conversation Dir of Eng T3 · Mgr development Week 2
5 E004 · Engineering Manager B Leadership skills Development conversation Dir of Eng T3 · Mgr development Week 2
6 E021 · ML Engineer A MLOps (gap 2.80) Stretch + external mentor pairing Staff Eng T4 · IC growth Week 2
7 E022 · ML Engineer B LLM foundations Program enrollment + stretch Staff Eng T4 · IC growth Week 2
8 E008 · Account Executive (top rep) Ent Negotiation Coaching enrollment VP Sales T5 · Sales coaching Week 3
9 E009 · Account Executive (new) Strategic Account Mgmt Coaching enrollment VP Sales T5 · Sales coaching Week 3
10 E015 · Senior Engineer (1-3 yr cohort) Retention risk + stretch Retention conversation Eng Manager T4 · IC growth Week 3
11 E016 · Senior Engineer (1-3 yr cohort) Retention risk + stretch Retention conversation Eng Manager T4 · IC growth Week 3
12 E017 · Product Manager Hypothesis Testing Workshop enrollment CPO T4 · IC growth Week 4
13 E031 · Onboarding new hire (0-1 yr) Role expectations recalibration Check-in + updated IDP Eng Manager T6 · New hire Week 4
14 E032 · Onboarding new hire (0-1 yr) Role expectations recalibration Check-in + updated IDP Senior Eng T6 · New hire Week 4
15 E042 · Long-tenured (5+ yr) Expectation drift Re-contracting conversation Dir of Eng T7 · Veteran reset Week 5
16 E043 · Long-tenured (5+ yr) Expectation drift Re-contracting conversation VP Sales T7 · Veteran reset Week 5

Conversation Type Mix — Q1 Workload
7 templates · 17 conversations
Manager Readiness for Dev Conversations
Not every manager can run a dev talk · 58% ready
Conversation Scripts — The Words You Actually Use
Template T1-T7 · opening · middle · closing
T1 · Executive Coaching Intake (Dir of Eng, VP Sales)
Open: "We've done a capability review across the senior leadership layer and I want to share what the assessment surfaced for your role, and then talk about what we'd like to do about it."

Middle: Name the specific gaps (Technical Depth, Strategic Thinking) with business impact language, not skill-deficit language. Present executive coaching as investment, not remediation. Share the 15.1x Return on Investment (ROI) framing.

Close: "We'd like to pair you with an external executive coach for 9 weeks starting in week 2. The goal is not to fix anything — it is to accelerate what you are already doing. Can you commit 90 minutes a week for this?"
T2 · Expert Retention (Sr DevOps Lead)
Open: "I want to have a direct conversation about your role here. The honest version is that you hold three capabilities that nobody else in the company holds, and the company is aware of that concentration and wants to do two things about it."

Middle: Present the retention commitment (stock refresh, explicit career pathing) first. Then present the knowledge-transfer ask second, so it does not feel like a replacement plan. Name the contractor + shadowing program and position it as leverage for them, not dilution of their role.

Close: "What I need from you is a named successor across each of the 3 capabilities over the next 6 months. What I am committing in return is [retention package]. Does this feel like a fair trade?"
T3 · Manager Development (Eng Mgrs, Staff Eng)
Open: "I want to talk about your development path into the next level. You're already delivering at the bar for your current role. This conversation is about what happens next."

Middle: Introduce the L4→L5 promotion gate explicitly as a feature, not a blocker. Explain that closing the listed gaps produces a durable promotion that sticks, versus an early promotion that carries forward debt. Share the monotonic gap growth chart if they are data-oriented.

Close: "We'd like to enroll you in the Leadership Development Cohort starting Q2. It is 24 weeks and includes a peer group of 10 others. Are there constraints in your current load I should know about before I commit you?"
T4 · IC Growth (ML Engs, Senior Engs, PMs)
Open: "I want to talk about your growth path over the next 12 months, and I have something specific in mind that I think you'd be excited about."

Middle: For the 1-3 year cohort, lead with stretch and visibility — not with gap language. They are performing well and are flight-prone; the conversation should feel like investment in their upside, not repair of their downside. Name the specific program (P01, P02, or P05) with a clear outcome. Offer a concrete stretch project attached to the program.

Close: "What I'd like to do is enroll you in [program] and pair it with [stretch]. That is what I can offer on the visible side. What else would make this year feel like growth rather than just delivery?"
T5 · Sales Coaching (AEs)
Open: "I want to connect with you about the FY25 enterprise push and the kind of support we're putting around it."

Middle: Frame P03 Enterprise Negotiation program as investment in the FY25 pipeline, not remediation of prior performance. The coaching is positioned as a resource the top reps get, not a fix for the bottom reps. Share deal-outcome Kirkpatrick L4 measurement upfront so it does not feel like a soft skills intervention.

Close: "We're pairing you with an external exec coach for 9 weeks, 1:1. The measurement is deal outcomes on specific named accounts we'll pick together in week 1. Is there a specific account you want to bring in as the focus?"
T6 & T7 · New Hire + Veteran Reset
T6 (New hire): "The onboarding materials you used in your first 90 days are being rewritten because the role changed. I want to make sure you have the updated picture of what the role actually expects before your next check-in."

T7 (5+ year): This is the hardest conversation on the list. Opening: "I want to have a re-contracting conversation about your role. The role has evolved while you've been doing it, and we have not done a good job of updating our expectations with you. I'd like to fix that now, and I want to start by being honest about what changed."

T7 Close: "What I am asking is that we re-write your role expectations together over the next 30 days. What I am committing is that we will invest in the development needed to close any new expectations that you did not sign up for. This is a shared fix, not a one-sided one."
HRBP Summary — 250 Words on Named Individuals
The one-person-at-a-time read

Where aggregation ends and people begin. The 11 critical gaps translate into 17 priority conversations in Q1 FY25. Two are executive coaching intakes with the Director of Engineering and VP Sales. One is a retention and knowledge-transfer conversation with the Senior DevOps Lead in Bangalore who singlehandedly holds three infrastructure capabilities. Four are manager development conversations with the Engineering Management layer that sits in the capability inversion. Eight are individual contributor conversations across ML Engineers, Senior Engineers, Product Managers, and Account Executives. Two are veteran re-contracting conversations with 5+ year employees whose role definitions drifted.

Manager readiness is the constraint. Running 17 development conversations depends on 12 managers being ready to run them, and current assessment puts manager readiness for development conversations at 58%. Five of the 12 managers are themselves in the gap (they are on the conversation list as employees, not just as conversation runners). The HRBP role is to run the conversations the managers cannot, and to prepare the managers who can.

The 7 Individual Development Plan (IDP) templates (Exec, Expert Retention, Mgr Dev, IC Growth, Sales Coaching, New Hire, Veteran Reset) cover every conversation type on the Q1 list. The templates are not scripts — they are scaffolds that force the conversation to surface the three things that matter: the specific gap in business-outcome language, the named program or intervention offered, and the explicit commitment the employee is asked to make in return.

HRBP priorities for Q1: (1) run conversations 1, 2, 3 in Week 1 — these are the 3 most time-sensitive and require HRBP presence not just manager presence, (2) train the 7 ready managers on the Individual Development Plan (IDP) templates before Week 2, (3) run conversations 4-9 in Weeks 2-3 with manager pairing, (4) run conversations 10-17 in Weeks 3-6 with manager primary ownership.

AI Insight — HRBP View
The HRBP view is the one that converts the 16 analytical cuts from Gap Analysis into the one thing every other view cannot produce: a specific roster of named individuals with a specific conversation to have this week. Every other view in this dashboard aggregates. This one disaggregates, and that is the point. The single most important observation is that 17 priority conversations map to 12 managers, and 5 of those 12 managers are themselves on the conversation roster as employees. That is the definition of a bootstrapping problem — the people who would normally run the conversations cannot run them because they need the conversations themselves. The HRBP role becomes temporary load-bearing for a quarter: running conversations the managers cannot yet run, pairing with the managers who can, and training the ones who need to become ready. The second observation is the 7-template structure: not every conversation is the same conversation. An executive coaching intake with the Director of Engineering is a fundamentally different interaction than a retention conversation with a 1-3 year Senior Engineer, which is different again from a re-contracting conversation with a 5+ year veteran whose role drifted. Treating them as "development conversations" and using the same opening for all 17 is the most common HRBP mistake and the one that produces template fatigue in employees. The 7 Individual Development Plan (IDP) templates force the conversation to be fit-for-purpose. The third observation is sequencing: conversations 1, 2, 3 must happen in Week 1 before any of the program rollouts begin. These are the ones that are load-bearing for the rest of the plan. Getting them wrong, or skipping them, or compressing them into 30-minute slots, produces cascading confusion across the remaining 14. Recommended HRBP sequencing: Week 1 conversations 1-3 with full HRBP presence and 60-minute slots minimum. Week 2 template training for the 7 ready managers. Weeks 2-3 conversations 4-9 with manager pairing. Weeks 3-6 conversations 10-17 with manager primary ownership and HRBP check-ins.
⚙️
L&D Ops View — Delivery Readiness Assessment
L&D Operations · Program Manager · Facilitator Sourcing · Learning Management System Operations (LMS Ops) · Vendor Mgmt
What this view optimizes for: assessing whether the L&D infrastructure is ready to deliver against the 11 identified gaps — not designing programs (that is TNI), but diagnosing the delivery constraints that will shape what programs are even feasible. The question this view answers: "What is our current L&D delivery capacity, what dependencies must be resolved before any intervention can start, and what is the realistic absorption window for the learning load these gaps represent?"
L&D Delivery Capacity
6 max
Concurrent programs · single ops lead
Structural Prerequisites
4
Must resolve before learning can land
External Vendor Dependency
4 gaps
No internal experts · sourcing required
Change Absorption Capacity
62%
Org readiness for concurrent learning load
L&D Infrastructure Readiness Assessment
Current state · gap vs requirement · readiness per component
Infrastructure gap summary: Learning Management System (LMS) platform is operational but onboarding content is obsolete (FY23 curriculum against FY25 roles). Vendor network exists for 2 of the 4 external-dependency gaps; the other 2 (Large Language Model (LLM) / Gen AI facilitation, Enterprise Negotiation coaching) require new vendor sourcing. Internal expert capacity is the most constrained resource — the 3 available L4+ experts who could facilitate technical depth learning already carry full delivery workloads, meaning any internal facilitation requires protected-time policy before any learning design begins.
Pre-Learning Dependency Map
What must happen before each gap cluster can be addressed
Critical dependencies: 5 of 9 learnable gap clusters are blocked by either a structural intervention (OD-owned) or a vendor sourcing requirement (L&D Ops-owned). The dependency chain means the first 4–8 weeks of any intervention phase are primarily dependency-resolution work, not learning delivery.
Organisational Absorption Capacity by Quarter
Max concurrent learning load vs gap demand · stagger requirement
Absorption constraint: the 9 learnable gaps map to 38 employees. Running all gaps simultaneously would require 38 employees to be in active learning across multiple domains while also delivering FY25 commitments. At 62% change readiness, the realistic absorption ceiling is 4–5 concurrent learning tracks per quarter. This is not a program design decision — it is a TNA finding that the Training Needs Identification (TNI) stage must respect.
Gap-Level Delivery Readiness — Constraints Per Gap
Infrastructure · dependencies · earliest feasible start · readiness rating
Gap Capability Need Delivery Dependency Infrastructure Status Earliest Feasible Start Readiness
G01 LLM / Gen AI External vendor sourcing (0 internal experts) Learning Management System (LMS) ready · no vendor Week 5–6 (post-RFP) Blocked — vendor
G02 MLOps External vendor sourcing (0 internal experts) Learning Management System (LMS) ready · no vendor Week 5–6 (post-RFP) Blocked — vendor
G03 Kubernetes Partial internal · contractor confirmation needed Learning Management System (LMS) ready · partial vendor Week 3–4 Near-ready
G04 Cloud Architecture Partial internal · cert path vendor selection Learning Management System (LMS) ready · partial vendor Week 4–5 Near-ready
G05 Strategic Leadership S1: Promotion gate redesign (OD · 8 wks)
S2: Role definition refresh (OD · 6 wks)		 Coaching vendor exists · structure not ready Week 9–10 (post S1+S2) Blocked — structural
G06 Enterprise Negotiation External exec coach sourcing No specialist vendor contracted Week 4–5 (post-coach selection) Blocked — vendor
G07 Technical Depth S4: Protected expert time policy (OD · 4 wks) Internal experts exist · no protected time Week 5 (post S4) Blocked — structural
G08 Hypothesis Testing Internal data team scheduling only Fully ready Week 1–2 Ready
G09 System Design Staff Eng availability + partial external Mostly ready Week 3–4 Near-ready
G10 Strategic Accounts Sales enablement vendor sourcing No specialist vendor contracted Week 5–6 (post-vendor) Blocked — vendor
G11 Onboarding Curriculum S3: Curriculum rewrite (OD + L&D · 10 wks) Learning Management System (LMS) ready · content obsolete Week 10–11 (post S3) Blocked — content
Learner Demand vs L&D Delivery Capacity
38 employees · 9 learnable gaps · quarterly absorption ceiling
L&D Ops Summary — Delivery Readiness in 250 Words
The infrastructure and constraint read

Delivery readiness position. Of the 9 learnable gaps, 3 are near-ready or ready for immediate intervention (G03, G04, G08, G09). Five are blocked by either vendor sourcing requirements (G01, G02, G06, G10) or structural OD interventions (G05, G07, G11). The blocked gaps account for 7 of the 9 learnable gap clusters, meaning the first phase of any intervention program is dependency-resolution, not learning delivery. This is the L&D Ops finding that most often gets omitted from capability reports — and its omission is why programs launch late.

Infrastructure constraints. The Learning Management System (LMS) platform is operational. The critical infrastructure gap is in content (G11 onboarding curriculum is obsolete) and in vendor network (4 gaps have no contracted external partner). Internal expert capacity is the hardest constraint: 3 L4+ engineers who could facilitate technical learning currently carry full delivery workloads. Protected-time policy (OD structural intervention S4) is a prerequisite for using them as facilitators — without it, their availability cannot be guaranteed at the point of program design in TNI.

Absorption ceiling. The 9 learnable gaps involve 38 employees across multiple roles. The organisation's change readiness score of 62% and the concurrent FY25 delivery commitments set a realistic absorption ceiling of 4–5 active learning tracks per quarter. This ceiling is a TNA finding — it is a constraint the Training Needs Identification (TNI) stage must design within, not a scheduling preference.

L&D Ops handoff to Training Needs Identification (TNI): 3 gaps ready to design now · 4 gaps blocked on vendor sourcing (lead time 4–6 wks) · 2 gaps blocked on structural OD work (lead time 8–10 wks) · absorption ceiling of 4–5 tracks per quarter · internal expert availability contingent on OD structural intervention S4.

AI Insight — L&D Ops View
The L&D Ops contribution to the TNA stage is delivery constraint intelligence — not scheduling, not logistics, but the honest assessment of what the delivery system can and cannot do against these gaps before the Training Needs Identification (TNI) stage designs programs around assumptions that turn out to be false. The single most important finding is the dependency structure: 7 of 9 learnable gaps have a pre-condition that is not in L&D Ops control. Four depend on vendor relationships that do not yet exist. Three depend on structural OD work that has not started. Designing programs for these gaps before the dependencies resolve is a known failure pattern — it produces curriculum that gets shelved because the precondition never arrived. The absorption ceiling is the second critical finding: 38 employees in active learning across multiple domains while simultaneously delivering FY25 commitments is not the same as 38 employees who are free to learn. The 62% change readiness score and the concurrent delivery pressure set a realistic ceiling of 4–5 learning tracks per quarter. Programs designed above this ceiling will see drop-out, partial completion, and poor behavioral transfer — not because the design was wrong, but because the system was overloaded. The internal expert constraint is the third: the 3 L4+ engineers available for internal facilitation are the most valuable and most fragile resource in the delivery system. Their availability requires a protected-time policy that is an OD structural intervention, not an L&D scheduling decision. L&D Ops diagnostic conclusion: dependency resolution is the critical path. The Training Needs Identification (TNI) stage should sequence program design around what the delivery system can actually absorb, starting with the 3 ready gaps and building vendor/structural unblocking in parallel.
Cross-View Comparison — The Same $1.06M Through All 6 Role Lenses
Same Data · 6 Decision Frames
How each role reads the same $1.06M investment
💼 C-SUITE reads:
"A $1.06M capital deployment with 4.5x avoided-cost ROI and 2.6-quarter payback. Four initiatives need re-timing for capability feasibility. Board-ready ask."
Decision: approve envelope + re-time roadmap
👥 CHRO reads:
"$680k annual attrition cost closes for $45k of coaching. 12 bus-factor exposures to protect. Promotion mechanic at L4→L5 is broken and must be fixed structurally. Protect the 19-person 1-3 year cohort."
Decision: approve coaching, install promotion gate, deploy retention levers
🎓 CLO reads:
"9 learnable gaps, 38-person learning population, 4 external-dependency gaps with zero internal experts. 6 gaps require behavior change or skill-build — not knowledge transfer. 2 structural prerequisites before 2 gaps can even be designed."
Decision: classify learning types, flag external dependencies, confirm structural prerequisites — then hand to Training Needs Identification (TNI)
🧭 OD HEAD reads:
"3 of 11 gaps are structure problems disguised as training needs. 4 structural interventions required before training lands. Change readiness at 62% — stage the rollout. Bus factor tolerance is a culture signal that needs policy."
Decision: sequence S1-S4 before training, stage rollout
🤝 HRBP reads:
"17 priority conversations in Q1, mapped to 12 managers, 5 of whom are themselves in the gap. 7 Individual Development Plan (IDP) templates covering every conversation type. Conversations 1-3 in Week 1 with full HRBP presence."
Decision: run Week-1 conversations, train the 7 ready managers
⚙️ L&D OPS reads:
"Delivery capacity ceiling: 6 concurrent tracks. 7 of 9 gaps are blocked — 4 on vendor sourcing, 3 on structural OD work. Only 3 gaps are ready for immediate intervention design. Absorption ceiling: 4–5 tracks per quarter at 62% change readiness."
Decision: map all dependencies, confirm absorption ceiling, identify ready gaps for Training Needs Identification (TNI) fast-track
The one-sentence synthesis: six roles look at the same 11 critical capability gaps through six different diagnostic lenses — C-Suite sees $4.8M in business cost and 4 infeasible FY25 initiatives; CHRO sees $680k in attrition and a broken promotion mechanic; CLO sees 9 learnable gaps, 4 external-dependency constraints, and 2 structural prerequisites; OD Head sees 3 structural design flaws and a 62% change readiness ceiling; HRBP sees 17 named development conversations and 38 Individual Development Plans (IDPs) to initiate; L&D Ops sees 7 blocked gaps and a 4–5 track quarterly absorption ceiling. All six converge on the same 11 gaps. What they each bring to the next stage — Training Needs Identification — is the specific lens, constraint, and decision authority their role contributes to converting this diagnosis into an executable intervention design.
TNA Complete — 6 Role Views · 11 Critical Gaps · Diagnostic Foundation for Training Needs Identification (TNI)
Training Needs Identification — Discovery Layer
10 signal sources · evidence-graded · what needs emerge from each lens before consolidation · FY25
Level 3 10 signal sources TNI Complete — all 10 sources + unified catalog
TNI

Training Needs Identification — The Methodologically Honest Discovery Layer

Every learning need has an origin · TNI traces needs back to their source and grades the evidence · no single source is trustworthy · triangulation is the point

10 sources
Need = Signal × Evidence Quality × Business Relevance Discovery precedes prescription Triangulation required · single-source findings are directional only
The Three-Stage Capability Closure Chain
TNA → TNI → Training Roadmap · each stage has a distinct purpose
Stage 1 · TNA — Training Needs Analysis (previous section)
Question: "What capability gaps exist, how severe are they, what do they cost, and what is their root cause?"

Organised by: 6 role-specific diagnostic lenses — C-Suite, Chief Human Resources Officer (CHRO), Chief Learning Officer (CLO), OD Head, Human Resources Business Partner (HRBP), Learning & Development Ops

Output: 11 critical gaps, classified by type (skill / competency / structural), with business impact, root cause, and learner population.

Role: Pure diagnosis — answers "what gaps exist and why"
✓ Complete — feeds into this section
Stage 2 · TNI — Training Needs Identification (this section)
Question: "Across all available evidence sources, which of the TNA gaps are confirmed learning needs — and how confident are we in each?"

Organised by: 10 independent signal sources — assessment data, business strategy, role drift, performance data, attrition, customer feedback, compliance, career aspiration, peer benchmarks, technology change

Output: 38 deduplicated needs, evidence-graded A–D, with triangulation scores. 11 confirmed critical needs ready to hand to the Roadmap.

Role: Evidence validation — answers "which needs are real and defensible"
← You are here
Stage 3 · Training Roadmap (next section)
Question: "For the confirmed needs, what programs should be designed, how should they be delivered, to whom, at what cost, in what sequence?"

Organised by: program catalog, delivery schedule, quarterly cohort loading, budget phasing, facilitator sourcing, dependency management, measurement plan

Output: A fully sequenced learning program portfolio with 30/60/90 day launch plan, budget envelope, and success metrics.

Role: Prescriptive planning — answers "what programs, when, and how"
→ Next — receives TNI hand-off
Most organisations collapse all three stages into one step and jump straight from "we have a skills problem" to "let's buy a training program." This produces programs that address symptoms rather than root causes, and budgets reapproved every year without narrowing the actual gap. The three-stage separation — diagnose first, validate second, design third — is what makes the difference between capability investment and capability theater.
Evidence Quality Grading — Used Across All 10 Sources
How to read every needs table in this section
A · High confidence
Multi-source, recent, validated by outcome data. Safe to act on without triangulation.
B · Medium confidence
2 sources agree or 1 high-quality source. Direction reliable, magnitude uncertain ±30%.
C · Directional only
Single source, moderate quality. Useful as a hypothesis to triangulate, not a planning input.
D · Weak / inferred
Self-reported only, stale, or inferred from absence of data. Needs validation before use.
10 Signal Sources — All Live
1. Assessment
A · from Gap Analysis
2. Business Strategy
B · from roadmap
3. Role Drift
C · definition vs actual
4. Performance Data
B · reviews + PIPs
5. Attrition
C · exit data
6. Customer / Delivery
B · outside-in signal
7. Compliance / Regulatory
A · non-negotiable floor
8. Career Aspiration
D · demand predictor
9. Peer Benchmark
B · comparative lens
10. Tech / Tooling Change
B · forced-function source
Source 1 · Assessment-Driven Needs — The Highest-Confidence Source
Source Overview · Assessment Data
Grade A · 58 skills × 25 competencies × 50 people × 5 evidence types
What the source captures:
Self-assessment + manager assessment + peer 360° + work-sample tests + outcome-based validation (deal close rates, code review scores, reliability SLOs). The foundation layer of everything else in the dashboard.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Self-assessment skew (30% of items self-only · VCS 54%). Manager halo effect on high performers. Work-sample tests age quickly. Outcome data is high-confidence but narrow (only works for roles with measurable outputs).
Needs Surfaced
41
Before criticality filter
Critical Subset
11
Business-blocking
Evidence Grade
A
Foundation layer
VCS Coverage
54%
Multi-source evidence
Needs by Assessment Source Type
Which evidence type surfaced the need
Confidence Distribution of Surfaced Needs

Top 10 Assessment-Driven Needs
Item · evidence sources · grade · triangulation status
# Need Primary Evidence Corroborating Sources Grade Directional or Actionable?
1LLM / Gen AIRole requirement inferenceNone — 0% assessment coverageDInferred from absence · needs validation
2MLOpsSelf-reportNone — not in peer/test dataDDirectional only · 30% confidence
3KubernetesSelf + managerInfra team self-reportCDirectional · 65% confidence
4Enterprise NegotiationSelf + manager + deal outcomesWin rate data, deal cycle timeAActionable · 85% confidence
5AWS ArchitectureSelf + manager + cert statusCertification recordsAActionable · 80% confidence
6Strategic Thinking (L5-L6)Self + manager + peer 360°3-source triangulationBActionable · 70% confidence
7Technical DepthSelf + manager + code reviewCode review scoresAActionable · 90% confidence
8Strategic Account MgmtSelf-report onlyNone — stale, 8 months oldDNeeds revalidation · 25% confidence
9System DesignSelf + manager + interview loopArchitecture review scoresAActionable · 82% confidence
10Hypothesis TestingSelf-report onlyNoneDNeeds revalidation · 30% confidence
AI Insight — Source 1 Assessment
Assessment is the highest-confidence source in the TNI catalog and also the most misleading when read alone. The source grade is A at the category level because the assessment machinery is formally run and produces defensible numbers, but confidence varies dramatically item by item within the 41 needs surfaced. Five items (Ent Negotiation, AWS, Tech Depth, System Design, Strategic Thinking) sit in the high-confidence band and can be actioned directly. Five other items (LLM, MLOps, Strat Account Mgmt, Hypothesis Testing, Systems Thinking) sit in Grade D — single-source, stale, or inferred from absence — and acting on them without triangulation is the most common TNI error. The LLM row is the most instructive: the reported gap of 3.20 is actually a calculated distance from role requirement to absent measurement, because no assessment has ever been run on LLM capability. Calling that a "gap" is technically accurate but epistemically generous. Assessment data should be the foundation layer, not the only layer. The next 4 sources in this section exist precisely to triangulate the 5 Grade D items and either confirm or correct them.
Source 2 · Business Strategy-Driven Needs — Forward-Looking Demand
Source Overview · Business Strategy Signals
Grade B · roadmap + strategic plan + investor commitments
What the source captures:
FY25 product roadmap, Series C commitments, IPO narrative requirements, new market entries, technology bets. Every strategic commitment implies capability requirements that may or may not exist in the current inventory.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Strategy documents overstate readiness (they are authored to inspire, not audit). Roadmap dates optimistic. Capability requirements often inferred rather than explicit. Useful as a demand signal, not a supply assessment.

7
FY25 pre-IPO pipeline
New Skills Required
14
Derived from initiatives
Emerging Skills (not in inventory)
6
Invisible to current system
Source Grade
B
Demand-side reliable
Initiative → Skill Requirement Map
7 initiatives · coverage % · gap severity
Emerging Skills Not Yet in Inventory
The silent layer · 6 skills
Strategy-Derived Needs — 7 Initiative Drivers
Initiative · timeline · skills implied · coverage · confidence
Initiative Timeline Skills Implied Currently in Inventory? Coverage % Grade
Gen AI Module GAQ2 FY25LLM, RAG, Prompt Eng, Vector DB1 of 428%B
Cloud Migration Phase 2Q3 FY25Terraform Adv, K8s, Service Mesh2 of 340%A
Enterprise GTMQ1 FY25Ent Negotiation, Strat Accounts2 of 2 (under-proficient)35%A
Global Expansion EMEAQ4 FY25Localization, Cross-Cultural Selling, Regional Ops0 of 315%C
Platform Re-ArchitectureQ3 FY25System Design, Event Sourcing2 of 255%A
SOC2 / ISOQ2 FY25Security, Compliance, IAM2 of 3 (BF=1)50%B
IPO ReadinessQ4 FY25IR Comms, Financial Modeling, Board Comms1 of 325%B
AI Insight — Source 2 Business Strategy
Strategy-driven needs surface 14 new skill requirements across 7 initiatives, 6 of which are not yet in the 58-skill inventory at all. This is the "silent gap" problem — needs that exist in demand but have no supply-side measurement system. RAG, Vector DBs, Prompt Engineering, Agent Orchestration, Cultural Fluency EMEA, and IR Communication will register as 100% gaps the moment they enter the inventory, which will make the dashboard look like it suddenly got worse in Q1 FY25 even though the reality has been present all along. The asymmetric risk: Source 1 (assessment) cannot see these needs because they have never been assessed. Source 2 sees them clearly because they come from the roadmap document. Any capability plan built on Source 1 alone would miss 6 of 14 strategy-implied needs and spend the subsequent year discovering them as program slips. Recommended Source 2 action: add the 6 emerging skills to the inventory in Q1 FY25 even though no employee has yet been assessed on them — making them visible in the system is the precondition for tracking them. This revalidation sprint should be scoped and costed as an early action item in the Training Roadmap planning stage.
Source 3 · Role Drift-Driven Needs — The Role Definition vs Actual Work Gap
Source Overview · Role Definition Drift
Grade C · directional · job description vs observed work
What the source captures:
Difference between formal role definition (job description, role card, competency matrix) and observed actual work (time studies, output analysis, manager interviews). When role definitions drift from reality, employees are effectively unpaid for half of what they do and untrained for all of it.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Observed-work data rarely collected systematically. Role definitions almost always outdated at high-growth companies. This source surfaces real needs but with low precision — it tells you where to look, not what to do.
Roles With Drift > 30%
8 / 13
Definition vs actual work

34%
Of job content has drifted
Role Cards Last Updated
22 mo
Median · stale
Source Grade
C
Directional only
Role Drift % by Role
13 roles · definition vs observed work overlap
Drift Growth Over Time
Last 3 years · drift accumulating without refresh
Top Drifted Roles — What the Role Definition Misses
Role · drift % · what's missing from the current card
Role Drift % In Card But Not Done Done But Not In Card Card Last Updated Grade
Director of Engineering52%Direct code ownership, individual sprint commitsStrategic roadmap, cross-org dependencies, M&A diligence26 moC
VP Sales48%Mid-market closing, deal-level coachingBoard-level revenue narrative, IPO roadshow prep24 moC
Senior Engineer (new definition)42%Individual feature deliverySystem design, cross-team alignment, tech debt ownership28 moC
Product Manager38%Sprint scheduling, ticket triageStrategic framing, exec alignment, data analysis18 moC
Engineering Manager35%Individual 1:1s onlyTeam design, cross-team coordination, capability planning20 moC
CSM32%Ticket handlingExpansion motion, executive relationships14 moB
Data Engineer30%ETL maintenanceData product ownership, model serving16 moB
Account Executive28%Volume-based targetsStrategic account stewardship, multi-year planning12 moB
AI Insight — Source 3 Role Drift
Role drift is the source most likely to invalidate the other four in this pass, because if the role definition is wrong, every gap measured against it is wrong. 8 of 13 roles show more than 30% drift between their formal definition and their actual observed work, and the median role card was last updated 22 months ago. Two roles stand out: the Director of Engineering (52% drift) and VP Sales (48% drift) both spend roughly half their time on work that is not in their job description and have been unassessed on that half. When Source 1 reports a +2.84 skill gap for Director of Engineering, some of that gap is real capability deficit, and some is the natural consequence of measuring someone against a role card that described a job they stopped doing two years ago. The methodological implication is significant: roughly 30% of the "gap" reported in the top-line RCGS number may actually be a role-definition artifact rather than a capability deficit. This does not make the gap smaller — the work still has to be done and the skills still have to exist — but it changes the intervention. A drifted role needs a role redefinition and a new assessment baseline before training dollars land, not just a training program. This is why OD Head intervention S2 (role definition refresh for L5-L6) is a prerequisite for any leadership development intervention — without it, any coaching program runs against a stale role definition. Recommended Source 3 action: run a formal role-drift refresh on the 8 high-drift roles within Q1 FY25, reconcile against Source 1 assessment data, and re-baseline the RCGS calculation afterward. Expect the top-line number to move by ±20% after this exercise.
Source 4 · Performance Data-Driven Needs — Review Patterns + PIPs
Source Overview · Performance Review Signals
Grade B · 2 review cycles × 50 employees + PIP data + promotion outcomes
What the source captures:
Performance review text + numerical ratings over 4 cycles, Performance Improvement Plans (PIPs) and their themes, promotion decisions (approved and rejected), manager comments on growth areas. Themes that repeat across multiple reviews are high-confidence signals.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Manager halo effect, leniency bias, review inflation. PIPs surface extreme underperformance but miss the 70% of employees in the middle who are growing slowly. Promotion decisions disproportionately weight political factors. Most useful for pattern-matching, least useful for individual prescription.

7
Appeared in ≥ 5 reviews

3
6% of org · typical for stage
Promotion Rejections
4
Last 2 cycles · themed
Source Grade
B
Pattern-strong, noise-prone

"Needs to think more strategically"12 reviewsL5-L6, some L4Strategic Thinking (#6)A triangulated
"Technical depth inconsistent"9 reviewsSr Eng, Staff EngTechnical Depth (#7)A triangulated
"Struggles with cross-functional alignment"8 reviewsEng Mgrs, PMsSystems Thinking (#11)B
"Enterprise sales motion underdeveloped"6 reviewsAEs, VP SalesEnt Negotiation (#4)B
"Lacks data-driven decision making"6 reviewsPMs, some Eng MgrsHypothesis Testing (#10)B
"System design choices create debt"5 reviewsSr EngSystem Design (#9)B
"Struggles to develop their team"5 reviewsEng MgrsLeadership (new theme)B
AI Insight — Source 4 Performance Data
Performance review data serves as triangulation fuel for Source 1 assessment findings — the primary value is not that it surfaces new needs, but that it confirms or contradicts existing ones. The top three recurring review themes ("strategic thinking," "technical depth," "cross-functional alignment") map directly to the top three competency gaps in the TNA critical list (Strategic Thinking, Technical Depth, Systems Thinking), which raises the confidence on those items from Grade B to Grade A through triangulation. This matters because Strategic Thinking and Technical Depth were Grade B in Source 1 — independent corroboration from performance data means the finding is now safe to act on without further revalidation. The most interesting Source 4 finding is actually an absence: no performance review theme corresponds to LLM, MLOps, or the Gen AI skills. This is not because those needs don't exist — it is because performance reviews are written against current role responsibilities, and LLM work is not yet in anyone's role responsibilities. This confirms what Sources 1 and 2 already showed: LLM is a forward-looking demand not yet surfaced in any backward-looking measurement. Recommended Source 4 action: use the triangulation to upgrade Strategic Thinking and Technical Depth from Grade B to Grade A in the unified catalog, and flag for the L&D Ops team that performance review data will not help validate the LLM/MLOps needs — those require a different validation source.
Source 5 · Attrition-Driven Needs — Exit Interviews + Retention Patterns
Source Overview · Attrition Signals
Grade C · 8 exits in last 12 months + retention risk scoring
What the source captures:
Exit interview themes, stated reasons for leaving, tenure at exit, regretted vs non-regretted categorization, retention risk scores on current employees. Attrition is a lagging indicator — by the time it's measurable, the cause has been operating for 6-18 months.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Exit interviews are self-reported and political — people give safe answers. Small sample size (8 exits / 50 people / 12 months). Stated reasons often differ from real reasons. Better read as a pattern than a quote.
12-Month Exits
8
16% voluntary · typical

5 / 8
63% regretted · high
Top Exit Theme
"Growth"
5 of 8 cited it
Source Grade
C
Directional pattern
Exit Reasons — Stated vs Triangulated
What people said vs what the pattern suggests
Exits by Tenure Band
When in their tenure did people leave
Exit Pattern Decomposition
8 exits · themed · root cause · needs surfaced
Tenure Band Count Stated Reason Likely Root Cause Need Surfaced Grade
1-3 year cohort5"Better growth opportunity"Manager development + stretch project absenceManager capability + IDP structureB
3-5 year cohort2"Comp / market"Promotion stagnation from broken L4→L5 gatePromotion mechanic + career pathingC
0-1 year cohort1"Role mismatch"Onboarding debt + stale role cardOnboarding curriculum refreshC
AI Insight — Source 5 Attrition
Attrition is a Grade C source because the sample is small (8 exits) and the signal is noisy (exit interviews are political), but the pattern it surfaces is high-value precisely because it triangulates with Source 3 role drift and Source 1 assessment data. Five of eight exits came from the 1-3 year cohort and cited "growth opportunity" as the stated reason. This is the cohort the CHRO view flagged as the healthiest and most flight-prone. The pattern confirms what the static assessment data could only suggest: the org is losing the specific employees who had been developing best, and losing them because the next growth step inside the company does not exist at the quality they can get elsewhere. Attribution to "manager capability + IDP structure absence" is a probabilistic inference — the stated reason was "growth" but the underlying mechanism is that their managers could not offer growth in a durable form, and the broken L4→L5 promotion gate meant even those who stayed faced capped advancement. Two exits from the 3-5 year cohort cited "comp/market" which is the classic polite exit reason when the real issue is promotion blocked by an opaque or broken mechanism. This triangulates with the CHRO view's finding that promotion velocity is disconnected from development velocity. Recommended Source 5 action: run a quiet retention-risk conversation with the 19-person 1-3 year cohort before Q1 FY25 ends, specifically testing whether stretch projects, visible ownership, and accelerated career pathing could shift the departure pattern. This is HRBP work (conversation T4 in the HRBP view) and does not require additional investment beyond the time it takes.
Source 6 · Customer & Delivery Feedback — The Outside-In Signal
Source Overview · Customer & Delivery Feedback
Grade B · support tickets + NPS + CSM calls + incident reports + win/loss data
What the source captures:
Support ticket themes, NPS verbatims, CSM escalation patterns, incident postmortems, win/loss interview transcripts, renewal risk signals, feature request patterns. What customers are actually experiencing when they touch the product and the people delivering it.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Vocal-minority bias on support tickets and NPS. Win/loss interviews favor recency. CSM escalations catch the loudest accounts, not the most important ones. Incident reports surface technical failures but miss slow degradation. Best used for pattern detection across many signals, not individual quotes.

9
Appeared 10+ times FY24
P0/P1 Incidents FY24
14
4 traced to capability gap

6
Patterns · not quotes
Source Grade
B
Pattern-strong, noisy

9 themes · mapped to internal skill/competency
Incident Root-Cause Attribution
14 P0/P1 incidents · capability gap vs infra vs process

# Signal Source Type Frequency Capability Implied Grade

6"Data quality questions unanswered"Support + CSM14 mentionsData Modeling + Hypothesis TestingB
7"Security review takes forever"Win/loss + CSM6 deal delaysSecurity / IAM + SOC2 ComplianceC

9"EMEA support hours inadequate"CSM + ticket dataongoingEMEA hiring (non-training)B
AI Insight — Source 6 Customer Feedback
Customer feedback is the most underweighted source in most TNI exercises because it is noisy, vocal-minority biased, and easy to dismiss as "not our real customers." That dismissal is a mistake. Customer signals are the only source that captures capability gaps from outside the organization's own measurement machinery, which makes them uniquely valuable for triangulation. Three of the top nine signals (Integrations slow/unreliable, Enterprise proposals generic, Platform slow at scale) triangulate directly with already-known Source 1 assessment gaps and can be upgraded to Grade A. The other six either confirm Sources 2 and 3 or introduce new texture on known gaps. The most important finding in Source 6 is about P0/P1 incident attribution: of 14 serious incidents in FY24, 4 trace directly to capability gaps rather than infrastructure or process failures, and those 4 incidents together cost an estimated $340k in customer concessions, remediation work, and deal risk. This is not new money — it is already embedded in the $4.8M cost-of-gap total from the TNA C-Suite view — but Source 6 is the one that lets you attribute specific dollars to specific incidents, which is the kind of evidence that survives skeptical executive scrutiny. Recommended Source 6 action: establish a quarterly capability-attribution review on the incident pipeline. This is essentially free to run (the incident postmortems already happen) and it produces the most defensible data source in the entire TNI catalog for future cycles.
Source 7 · Compliance & Regulatory — The Non-Negotiable Floor
Source Overview · Compliance & Regulatory Signals
Grade A · SOC2 + GDPR + ISO + EMEA regulations + IPO filing requirements
What the source captures:
Required capabilities imposed by external standards and regulations: SOC2 Type II controls, GDPR privacy requirements, ISO 27001 for enterprise deals, financial reporting controls for IPO readiness, EMEA data residency rules, AI governance emerging standards. Unlike every other source, compliance needs are non-optional — they either exist or an audit fails.
Inherent biases to correct for:
Compliance frameworks overstate specificity (many controls are genuinely vague). Deadline-driven bunching (everything urgent in Q2 before audit). Least useful for culture or behavioral needs, most useful for codifiable skill requirements with hard dates.
Active Frameworks
5
SOC2 · ISO · GDPR · IPO · AI Gov

28
Of 147 total controls
Audit Deadline
Q2 FY25
SOC2 Type II · non-negotiable
Source Grade
A
Framework-defined

5 frameworks · % controls with required capability in place
